You are only a warner for those who fear it. Your job, O Prophet ﷺ, is not to tell people when it’s coming. Your job is to warn those who will genuinely be afraid of it.

Some people become obsessed with the signs of Judgment Day - analyzing symbols, looking for hidden meanings in current events. But what did the Prophet ﷺ teach about the signs? Even if the Dajjal appears while you’re planting a seed, what should you do? Finish planting the seed. Keep business as usual.

For a true believer, circumstances don’t change your commitment. Whether times are good or difficult, you continue doing what you’re supposed to do. That’s steadfastness.

May Allah ﷻ make us all genuinely afraid of that Day in a way that draws us closer to Him.

Ayah 46

كَأَنَّهُمْ يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَهَا لَمْ يَلْبَثُوٓا۟ إِلَّا عَشِيَّةً أَوْ ضُحَىٰهَا

It will be as though, on the Day they see it, they had not remained in the world except for an evening or a morning thereof.

The word يَرَوْنَهَا indicates physical sight - you will actually witness this event before your eyes. It’s not merely spiritual or metaphorical. You will physically see the Hour arrive.

When that moment comes, all of this life will feel like it passed in an instant - just one evening and the morning that followed. Think about it: most of the evening you spend sleeping. If you sleep from 10 PM to 6 AM, those eight hours pass in what feels like a second. One moment you’re reading something, the next moment you wake up. There’s nothing in between for you - no awareness of time passing.

Then the morning comes, and you’re in a rush to get ready for work, school, or whatever awaits you. Before you know it, the morning is gone. The quickest parts of the day, the portions that escape us fastest, are the night and the early morning. That’s precisely what Allah ﷻ highlights here.

The time you have in this life will pass so quickly. Don’t spend this life like someone who sleeps through the night, passed out and unaware. Don’t waste these precious moments in heedlessness. Wake up. Be conscious. Be prepared.

Because when you stand before Allah ﷻ, you’ll wish you had more time. But time will be up, and all you’ll have are the deeds you sent forward.

Ayah 1–11 · linked video: 01. An-Nazi’at (Ayah 1-11)

Root Word Analysis

Ayah 1

النَّازِعَاتِ (an-nazi’at) [ن-ز-ع]

The root ن-ز-ع means to pull, extract, or yank something out. When the form نَزَعَ is used, it indicates pulling something with force. The pattern نَازِعَات (active participle, feminine plural) describes those who are actively engaged in pulling. The addition of غَرْقًا (gharqan) intensifies this meaning significantly. In classical Arabic, نَزْعٌ غَرْقًا (naz’un gharqan) specifically means to pull something with maximum tension - like an arrow pulled back in a bow to its furthest extent, where you cannot pull it any further. This is maximum tension, the most you could possibly pull. The word غَرْق itself means drowning, and figuratively it’s used when something is completely immersed or when maximum effort is applied. In the context of horses, this describes the raiders holding back the reins with absolute tension, restraining the horses before the attack with every ounce of strength.

Ayah 2

النَّاشِطَاتِ (an-nashitat) [ن-ش-ط]

The root ن-ش-ط means to release, let loose, or untie something that was bound. The word نَشَطَ originally refers to a knot or something that holds things together but can easily be undone. When you نَشَطْتَ something, you’ve released it completely. The pattern نَاشِطَات (active participle, feminine plural) indicates those who are actively releasing or letting go. The addition of نَشْطًا emphasizes complete release - not a partial letting go but a total unleashing. In the imagery being developed, after holding the reins with maximum tension (Ayah 1), this describes the moment when the raiders release the reins entirely. The horses are no longer restrained - they’re completely unleashed and free to charge. The word choice emphasizes the contrast between the extreme holding back and the total release.

Ayah 3

السَّابِحَاتِ (as-sabihat) [س-ب-ح]

The root س-ب-ح fundamentally means to swim or float. It’s used for swimmers in water, boats floating on the sea, and even celestial bodies moving through space. The pattern سَابِحَات (active participle, feminine plural) describes those actively engaged in swimming. The addition of سَبْحًا intensifies the action - not just swimming but swimming with speed and purpose. When the form السَّابِحَاتِ سَبْحًا is used together, it emphasizes vigorous, piercing movement through a medium. Arabs would use this figuratively for horses running in the desert - the desert being like an ocean of land, and the horses “swimming” through it. When a boat or shark moves quickly through water, it leaves a wake behind, tearing through the water. Similarly, when horses run at full speed across the desert, they leave a trail, tearing through the land. This describes both the violence of the movement (piercing, tearing through) and the incredible speed (like flying). Now that the horses have been released, they’re charging down the hill with devastating speed and force.

Ayah 4

السَّابِقَاتِ (as-sabiqat) [س-ب-ق]

The root س-ب-ق means to precede, get ahead, or arrive first. There’s a distinction in Arabic between مُسَابِق (musabiq) which means racing or competing with others, and سَابِق (sabiq) which means getting there first, being the foremost. The pattern السَّابِقَاتِ سَبْقًا emphasizes not racing with each other but arriving at the destination immediately, with great speed. This is not about horses competing against one another for first place - that would be مُسَابِقَات. Rather, this describes a coordinated force that reaches its target swiftly and decisively. They had their target in sight, they moved with discipline and precision, and they arrived there in no time. The emphasis is on the speed of arrival and the coordinated nature of the attack - all the raiders reaching their positions simultaneously, exactly as planned.

Ayah 5

الْمُدَبِّرَاتِ (al-mudabbirat) [د-ب-ر]

The root د-ب-ر means to turn away, to go behind, or to consider what comes after. The word دُبُر means “behind” or “back” and the verb دَبَّرَ means to plan by thinking through consequences - to consider what’s behind or what will come after each action. The form تَدْبِير means both planning AND executing that plan. The pattern الْمُدَبِّرَاتِ (active participle, feminine plural) describes those actively engaged in planning and execution. The addition of أَمْرًا (amran - a matter/command) makes this comprehensive: those who plan the matter and execute it with perfect decision-making. This isn’t just making plans - it’s considering all possibilities, weighing consequences (if we do this, then that; if they do that, then this), and then carrying out the plan flawlessly. In the context of the raid, every raider knows exactly where to go, what to do, how long they have, what their exit strategy is. It’s a coordinated, disciplined operation where the plan has accounted for multiple scenarios and everyone executes their role perfectly. The raid wasn’t just successful because of speed - it was successful because it was meticulously planned and flawlessly executed.

Ayah 6

تَرْجُفُ (tarjufu) [ر-ج-ف]

The root ر-ج-ف means to shake, tremble, or quake violently. The verb رَجَفَ describes intense shaking or trembling. Linguistically, رَجْف is used for the kind of fever that makes someone shiver and shake uncontrollably. The form تَرْجُفُ (third person feminine singular, present tense) means “it shakes” or “it trembles.” What’s remarkable here is that Allah doesn’t say “the day the earth IS SHAKEN” (passive) but rather “the day the earth SHAKES” (active). The earth itself is the doer, the active agent. It’s not something being shaken by an external force - the earth itself is doing the shaking.

الرَّاجِفَةُ (ar-rajifah) [ر-ج-ف]

This is the active participle from the same root ر-ج-ف, meaning “the one that shakes” or “the shaker.” The pattern فَاعِلَة emphasizes that this is the nature and characteristic of the subject - it IS the shaker, not the shaken. This grammatical choice is deliberate and profound. Just as the horses wanted to move and the reins held them back, the earth wants to shake and Allah has been holding it back. The earth doesn’t disobey Allah, but it witnesses the disobedience of humanity on its surface. It wants to react to the sins committed on it, but Allah says “not yet.” On Judgment Day, Allah will release the reins - He’ll let the earth do what it has wanted to do. The earth itself becomes the attacker, the raider. You don’t need an external force - the threat is literally under your feet.

Ayah 7

تَتْبَعُهَا (tatba’uha) [ت-ب-ع]

The root ت-ب-ع means to follow. The form تَتْبَعُ (third person feminine singular, present tense) means “it follows” and the attached pronoun هَا means “it” (referring back to the first shake). The verb emphasizes immediate succession - something following right behind.

الرَّادِفَةُ (ar-radifah) [ر-د-ف]

The root ر-د-ف means to follow closely behind, to ride behind someone (like a second person on a horse or motorcycle), or to be a second wave. The pattern الرَّادِفَة (active participle) emphasizes that this is the follower, the one that comes right after. In classical Arabic usage, رَدِيف referred to the person riding in the back of a horse or camel. It also means a second wave that comes on top of the first wave. The grammatical structure here is critical: the first shake happens, and IMMEDIATELY following it comes the second. There’s no gap, no time to recover. When the first wave of the raid came, villagers might think “thank God it’s over” - but then the second wave hits immediately. Similarly, the first shaking brings people out of their graves in resurrection, and immediately following that comes the judgment. The rapid succession leaves no time to think, no time to prepare, no time to recover from the shock.

Ayah 8

قُلُوبٌ (qulub) [ق-ل-ب]

The root ق-ل-ب means to turn, flip, or change. The word قَلْب (qalb) literally means “that which turns” and it’s the Arabic word for heart - because the heart is constantly turning, changing, beating. The plural قُلُوبٌ refers to multiple hearts.

وَاجِفَةٌ (wajifah) [و-ج-ف]

The root و-ج-ف means to be disturbed, to tremble, to palpitate with fear. The pattern وَاجِفَة (active participle, feminine singular describing قُلُوبٌ) means hearts that are intensely disturbed, trembling, beating rapidly out of terror. This isn’t ordinary fear - this is the kind of deep disturbance that makes your heart race uncontrollably. The hearts on that day will be in a state of profound shock and terror, trembling from what they’re witnessing and what they know is coming.

Ayah 9

أَبْصَارُهَا (absaruha) [ب-ص-ر]

The root ب-ص-ر means to see, perceive, or have insight. The word بَصَر means sight or vision, and the plural أَبْصَار means eyes or sights. The attached pronoun هَا refers back to the hearts (قُلُوبٌ), creating a profound connection: “the eyes OF THOSE hearts” - not just eyes in general, but specifically the eyes of those terrified, trembling hearts.

خَاشِعَةٌ (khashi’ah) [خ-ش-ع]

The root خ-ش-ع means to be humble, submissive, or to lower oneself. However, خُشُوع has multiple dimensions. It can mean humility out of reverence and respect (positive), or it can mean humiliation and being brought low out of fear and shame (negative). The pattern خَاشِعَة (active participle, feminine singular describing أَبْصَار) emphasizes this is the state of the eyes. In this context, it specifically means eyes that are humiliated, unable to look up, cast downward in shame. These aren’t eyes of reverent humility - these are eyes that cannot make eye contact because they know what they’ve done. Like a child with a bad report card approaching their mother, unable to look up. The eyes tell the story of what the heart contains - broken, ashamed, terrified. The grammatical connection (أَبْصَارُهَا - the eyes of those hearts) means you can see from the look of the eyes that those hearts are broken and filled with shame.

Ayah 10

أَإِنَّا (a-inna) [همزة استفهام + إن]

This is a combination of أَ (the interrogative hamza indicating a question) and إِنَّا (verily we). This creates a question that carries shock and disbelief: “Are we really…?” or “Is it really that we…?” The form expresses incredulity.

لَمَرْدُودُونَ (la-mardudun) [ر-د-د]

The root ر-د-د means to return, send back, or restore to a previous state. The pattern مَرْدُودُون (passive participle, masculine plural) means “ones who are returned” or “ones who are thrown back.” The لَ at the beginning is for emphasis. The complete meaning is: “Are we really going to be returned/thrown back?” This carries two possible interpretations: (1) Said sarcastically in the worldly life - “You think we’re going to be brought back after death? Ridiculous!” (2) Said in shock on Judgment Day - “Are we really the ones being brought back? We were already dead, already gone. Why are we here?”

Ayah 11

الْحَافِرَةِ (al-hafirah) [ح-ف-ر]

The root ح-ف-ر means to dig or excavate. The word حُفْرَة means a pit, ditch, or hole dug in the ground. The pattern الْحَافِرَة refers to the state of being in a dug pit or grave. This is referring to the graves - the ditches in the ground where bodies are buried. The questioning continues from the previous ayah: “Will we be returned to the ditch?” or “Are we the ones who were in the ditch?” Again, this carries duality: (1) Mockingly in worldly life - “You think after we’re buried in graves we’ll come back out?” (2) In shock on Judgment Day - “We were already in the graves, already dead and buried. What are we doing here now?”

نَخِرَةٍ (nakhirah) [ن-خ-ر]

The root ن-خ-ر means to be hollow, decayed, or rotten. The word نَخِر specifically describes bones that have become hollow from the inside, brittle and easily broken. These are bones that have completely decayed, that are so old and deteriorated they make a hollow sound - like when you blow through a hollow bone and it makes a whistling sound. The pattern نَخِرَة (adjective, feminine singular describing عِظَامٍ - bones) emphasizes bones that are not just old but completely hollowed out, fragile, collapsing. The complete phrase أَإِذَا كُنَّا عِظَامًا نَخِرَةً means “When we have become bones that are hollow and decayed.” This emphasizes the impossibility being mocked: “After we’ve been dead so long that even our bones have turned to dust and hollow remains, you think we’ll be brought back?” But on Judgment Day, these same words will be said in shock: “We were reduced to such decay, to nothing but hollow bones, and yet we’re standing here now?”

Grammatical & Rhetorical Nuances

Ayahs 1-5 (The Oath Structure)

The opening five ayahs constitute a remarkable oath structure (qasam) that has generated extensive scholarly debate. Grammatically, these ayahs share a parallel structure: each begins with و (wa - and/by) followed by a feminine plural active participle (النَّازِعَاتِ، النَّاشِطَاتِ، السَّابِحَاتِ، السَّابِقَاتِ، الْمُدَبِّرَاتِ), and most are followed by a verbal noun (مصدر) for emphasis (غَرْقًا، نَشْطًا، سَبْحًا، سَبْقًا، أَمْرًا).

The use of active participles (اسم الفاعل) is rhetorically significant. Active participles describe ongoing, characteristic actions - these aren’t one-time events but habitual activities. The feminine plural form is used for non-human plurals in Arabic, which is why these can refer to horses, angels, stars, or other entities.

The addition of the verbal nouns (مصدر مؤكد) after each participle serves as تأكيد (emphasis). This is called المصدر المؤكد للفعل - an absolute object that intensifies the meaning of the verb. It’s like saying in English “they pulled with extreme pulling” or “they swam with intense swimming.” This rhetorical device amplifies each action to its maximum degree.

A critical grammatical issue is the absence of جواب القسم (the response to the oath). Normally, when Allah takes an oath (قسم), there’s a جواب القسم - the statement being sworn to. Classical examples show a clear structure: the oath followed by the point being emphasized. However, here the response is not immediately stated. Various scholars attempted to fill this gap:

  1. Some argued it’s understood: “I swear [you will be resurrected]”
  2. Others said the response appears later (Ayah 6 or beyond) with an implied لَ
  3. Some suggested reading the ayahs in reverse order
  4. Ibn Ashur and others proposed the ayahs should begin with فَ (fa)

Dr. Bint al-Shati’ presents a compelling counter-argument: not every qasam in the Quran follows a rigid grammatical formula. The Quran transcends and transforms Arabic grammatical rules. Sometimes an oath is taken purely to capture attention, and then the subject changes entirely. This is a rhetorical strategy - create intense anticipation through the oath, build dramatic tension, and then shift to the main message. The oath doesn’t need an explicit jawab because its function is to prepare the psychological state of the listener for what’s coming next.

Ayah 6-7 (Grammatical Choices)

The grammatical choice of الرَّاجِفَةُ (active participle, “the one that shakes”) rather than المَرْجُوفَةُ (passive participle, “the one that is shaken”) is profoundly significant. In Arabic grammar, the choice between active and passive voice shifts agency. By using the active participle, Allah makes the earth the subject, the doer of the action. The earth isn’t a passive victim being shaken by something else - the earth itself is the active agent doing the shaking.

This grammatical choice creates a psychological effect: the threat isn’t external, it’s literally beneath your feet. You can’t escape from the earth - you’re standing on the very thing that will attack you.

The verb تَرْجُفُ is in the present/future tense (المضارع), which in Arabic describes both habitual action and future events. Combined with يَوْمَ (on the day), it describes the future Day of Judgment.

The structure تَتْبَعُهَا الرَّادِفَةُ is masterful. The verb تَتْبَعُ is in the imperfect tense, indicating immediate, ongoing succession. The attached pronoun هَا refers back to الرَّاجِفَةُ, creating tight grammatical cohesion. The use of الرَّادِفَةُ (the one that follows right behind) with the definite article (ال) suggests this second event is known, expected, and inevitable - not just “a follower” but “THE follower,” THE second wave that everyone should know is coming.

This creates a grammatical mirror of the raid imagery: just as the villagers might think the attack is over after the first wave, only to be hit by a second wave, the grammatical structure suggests the first shake might seem like the end, but there’s more coming immediately after.

Ayah 8-9 (The Possessive Construction)

The structure of these ayahs uses إضافة (possessive construction) in a rhetorically powerful way: قُلُوبٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ وَاجِفَةٌ - أَبْصَارُهَا خَاشِعَةٌ. Literally: “Hearts on that day will be trembling - their eyes humiliated.”

The pronoun هَا in أَبْصَارُهَا refers back to قُلُوبٌ, creating the meaning “the eyes OF THOSE hearts.” This is a profound rhetorical connection (إيجاز - eloquent conciseness). Instead of saying “hearts will tremble and eyes will be humiliated,” Allah connects them: the eyes are specifically the eyes belonging to those trembling hearts. This emphasizes cause and effect - the eyes look the way they do BECAUSE of what the hearts contain. You can see the state of the heart by looking at the eyes.

This rhetorical device creates psychological insight: external appearance (eyes) directly reflects internal reality (heart). There’s no hiding, no pretending. The humiliation in the eyes reveals the terror in the heart.

Ayah 10-11 (Interrogative Rhetoric)

Both ayahs employ استفهام (interrogative rhetoric), but the function differs based on interpretation:

  1. As mockery (in the worldly life): These are استفهام إنكاري (interrogative for denial/mockery). The questions imply the negative answer: “You really think we’ll be brought back? Impossible!” This is a common Arabic rhetorical device where a question implies emphatic rejection.

  2. As shock (on Judgment Day): These become استفهام للتعجب (interrogative for astonishment). The questions express disbelief at what’s actually happening: “Are we really being brought back? This can’t be real!”

The genius of the Quranic rhetoric is that the SAME WORDS carry BOTH meanings simultaneously. In the worldly life, arrogant disbelievers mock resurrection using these words. On Judgment Day, those same people will use those EXACT SAME WORDS with completely opposite emotional content - not mockery but terror.

The repetition of أَ (interrogative hamza) at the start creates emphasis and incredulity. The structure أَإِنَّا لَمَرْدُودُونَ combines interrogative + emphatic particle (إِنَّ) + emphasis lam (لَ) creating layers of emphasis: “Are we REALLY going to be RETURNED?”

The phrase أَإِذَا كُنَّا عِظَامًا نَخِرَةً uses إِذَا (when/if) with كَانَ (to be) to express a hypothetical temporal condition: “When/if we have become hollow bones.” The condition emphasizes the impossibility being challenged - even after complete physical decay, resurrection will happen.

Historical & Biblical Context

Ayahs 1-5 (Pre-Islamic Arabian Context)

The imagery of nighttime raids by horseback warriors reflects a historical reality in pre-Islamic Arabia. Tribal warfare and raiding (غَزْو - ghazw) were common practices. Tribes would conduct surprise attacks on rival tribes or villages, typically at night, to steal resources, livestock, and valuables. These raids required careful planning, coordination, and the use of horses - the most valuable military asset of the time.

The Quraysh and other Arabian tribes had intimate familiarity with such raids. They lived in constant awareness of the possibility of attack. Villages and camps maintained watch systems, and the terror of a nighttime raid was a lived reality for the original audience of this surah.

This historical context explains why the opening imagery would have been immediately powerful and visceral to the original listeners. They didn’t need explanation - they could visualize exactly what was being described: the tension of held reins, the moment of release, the thundering charge, the coordinated attack, the shock of the sleeping village.

Ayah 6 (Eschatological Context)

The concept of a violent earthquake marking the Day of Judgment appears throughout Islamic eschatology and has parallels in other Abrahamic traditions. In Biblical literature, earthquakes are associated with divine judgment and the end times (see Ezekiel 38:19-20, Revelation 6:12, 16:18).

The Quran repeatedly describes apocalyptic earthquakes: Surah Al-Hajj (22:1-2) states “O mankind, fear your Lord. Indeed, the convulsion of the [final] Hour is a terrible thing. On the Day you see it every nursing mother will be distracted from that [child] she was nursing, and every pregnant woman will abort her pregnancy, and you will see the people [appearing] intoxicated while they are not intoxicated; but the punishment of Allah is severe.”

Surah Al-Zalzalah (99:1-2) describes: “When the earth is shaken with its [final] earthquake, and the earth discharges its burdens.” This surah (An-Nazi’at) contributes to this consistent Quranic theme of the earth’s violent transformation on Judgment Day.

Ayahs 10-11 (Makkan Context of Resurrection Denial)

These ayahs reflect the historical objections of the Quraysh to the doctrine of resurrection. The Makkan period of revelation was characterized by fierce opposition from the Quraysh elite, who rejected Muhammad’s ﷺ message, particularly the concept of life after death.

Throughout the Quran, their arguments are preserved: “When we are bones and crumbled particles, are we really to be resurrected as a new creation?” (17:49); “Who will give life to bones while they are disintegrated?” (36:78). The Quraysh found bodily resurrection philosophically absurd and scientifically impossible.

This surah is responding to actual historical objections being raised in Makkah. The mockers would use exactly these phrases - “hollow bones” - to ridicule the concept of resurrection. By quoting their words back to them and showing these same words will be said on Judgment Day with opposite meaning, Allah demonstrates both His awareness of their objections and the certainty that they will witness what they denied.

No specific Biblical parallels are mentioned in this passage, though the concept of resurrection exists in Jewish and Christian eschatology. The Quranic emphasis on bodily resurrection (bones, graves) distinguishes Islamic eschatology from some Christian interpretations focusing on spiritual resurrection.

Coherence

Connection Between the Oath and the Message (Ayahs 1-6)

The coherence of this surah is masterful once we understand the thematic connection between the opening raid imagery (Ayahs 1-5) and the Judgment Day description (Ayahs 6-11).

The raid serves as an earthly parallel to Judgment Day with multiple points of correspondence:

  1. Tension and Release: Just as the raiders hold the reins with maximum tension (النَّازِعَاتِ غَرْقًا) before releasing them (النَّاشِطَاتِ نَشْطًا), Allah has been restraining the earth from shaking in response to human sin. On Judgment Day, He releases that restraint - يَوْمَ تَرْجُفُ الرَّاجِفَةُ.

  2. Surprise Attack: The sleeping village has no awareness of the impending raid until it happens. Similarly, Judgment Day will come suddenly to those who denied it: “It will not come to you except unexpectedly” (7:187).

  3. Speed and Coordination: The raid happens with devastating speed (السَّابِحَاتِ سَبْحًا، السَّابِقَاتِ سَبْقًا) and coordination (الْمُدَبِّرَاتِ أَمْرًا). The resurrection and judgment will likewise happen swiftly and with perfect divine coordination.

  4. Two Waves: The structure of the raid likely involved multiple waves of attack. Similarly, Judgment Day has two shakes: الرَّاجِفَةُ followed immediately by الرَّادِفَةُ - possibly the earth shaking and then the resurrection, or the resurrection and then the gathering for judgment.

  5. Terror and Helplessness: The villagers wake in shock and confusion, unable to comprehend what’s happening. This mirrors قُلُوبٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ وَاجِفَةٌ أَبْصَارُهَا خَاشِعَةٌ - the terror and humiliation of those facing judgment.

This thematic coherence explains why Allah chose THIS particular imagery for THIS particular message. The oath isn’t random - it’s a carefully chosen parallel that makes the abstract concept of Judgment Day concrete and visceral.

Internal Coherence of the Surah

The surah moves from the oath (1-5) to the event (6-7) to the reaction (8-9) to the words spoken (10-11). This is a logical, narrative progression:

  • First, establish the frame of reference (raid imagery)
  • Second, describe the main event (earth shaking)
  • Third, show the human response (terror, humiliation)
  • Fourth, quote what people say (their questions and shock)

The progression builds psychological intensity. We move from external action (raid, earthquake) to internal experience (hearts trembling, eyes humiliated) to verbal expression (their words of shock).

Connection to Previous Surah (An-Naba)

Surah An-Naba’ (78), which immediately precedes An-Nazi’at, ends with the powerful statement: “Indeed, We have warned you of a near punishment on the Day when a man will observe what his hands have put forth and the disbeliever will say, ‘Oh, I wish that I were dust!’” (78:40)

The word “dust” (تُرَابًا) connects to “the ditch/grave” (الْحَافِرَةِ) in An-Nazi’at (79:10). The disbeliever’s wish to be dust - to remain dead - is immediately followed by a surah describing exactly why he’ll wish that: the terror of resurrection and judgment.

Both surahs deal with Judgment Day, but An-Naba’ focuses on the questions about it (“What are they asking about?”) while An-Nazi’at focuses on the sudden, shocking reality of its arrival.

Connection to Surah Themes Throughout the Quran

The concept of al-rajfah (violent shaking) connects to multiple Quranic passages: - Surah Al-Zalzalah (99:1): “When the earth is shaken with its earthquake” - Surah Al-Hajj (22:1): “The convulsion of the Hour is a terrible thing” - Surah Al-Waqi’ah (56:4-6): “When the earth is shaken with convulsion, and the mountains are broken down, crumbling, and become dust dispersing”

The mockery of resurrection (Ayahs 10-11) connects to numerous Makkan surahs where the Quraysh’s objections are addressed: Al-Isra’ (17:49), Ya-Sin (36:78), Al-Mu’minun (23:35), and others.

The theme of hearts trembling and eyes humiliated connects to descriptions of believers’ opposite state - hearts at peace and faces radiant (75:22-24, 80:38-39).

Psychological, Political & Social

Ayahs 1-5 (Psychology of Anticipation and Tension)

The opening ayahs create a masterful psychological effect through the imagery of restrained then released violence. The progression from pulling back (النَّازِعَاتِ غَرْقًا) to releasing (النَّاشِطَاتِ نَشْطًا) mirrors a fundamental human psychological experience: the building of tension followed by its release.

This has multiple psychological applications:

  1. Delayed Gratification: Just as the raiders must hold back despite their desire to attack, humans must often restrain their desires and impulses. The tension of self-control is palpable in this imagery.

  2. Anticipatory Anxiety: Those who deny the afterlife live in false security, like a sleeping village unaware of approaching danger. The psychological state of heedlessness (غَفْلَة) is being challenged.

  3. Cognitive Dissonance: The disbelievers mock resurrection while simultaneously having some inner anxiety about its possibility. This internal conflict is reflected in their emphatic denials - the more they protest, the more their unconscious uncertainty is revealed.

Ayahs 6-7 (The Psychology of Sudden Catastrophe)

These ayahs describe the psychological experience of sudden, overwhelming calamity. The two-wave structure (الرَّاجِفَةُ followed by الرَّادِفَةُ) reflects a known psychological phenomenon: when trauma comes in waves, there’s no recovery time between shocks. This compounds the psychological devastation.

The earth itself becoming the threat (الرَّاجِفَةُ as active agent) creates existential terror - where can you flee when the very ground beneath you is the danger? This mirrors the psychological experience of pervasive anxiety where there’s no safe place.

The sudden nature (no warning, no preparation) reflects how Judgment Day will catch many in psychological denial. They’ve suppressed the reality so thoroughly that its arrival will be cognitively shattering.

Ayahs 8-9 (Psychology of Shame and Humiliation)

These ayahs describe two interconnected psychological states:

  1. قُلُوبٌ وَاجِفَةٌ (Trembling hearts): This describes acute anxiety, terror, the physiological response to extreme fear. The heart races, pounds, and seems to jump in the chest. This is the physical manifestation of existential dread - knowing you’re about to face accounting for your life.

  2. أَبْصَارُهَا خَاشِعَةٌ (Their eyes humiliated): This describes shame - the inability to make eye contact, the downcast gaze of someone who knows they’ve done wrong. Unlike fear (which might make eyes wide with terror), shame makes eyes unable to look up.

The connection between these (“the eyes OF those hearts”) is psychologically profound: external behavior reveals internal state. You cannot hide shame - it shows in body language, particularly in the eyes. Modern psychology confirms that shame and guilt are visible in facial expressions and eye contact patterns.

The Quranic description anticipates by 1,400 years what psychology has confirmed: the eyes are windows to emotional states, and shame specifically manifests in gaze aversion.

The comparison to a student with a bad report card is apt because it captures a universal human experience of shame in the face of authority when you know you’ve failed. That feeling - the inability to look up, the desire to disappear - will be the overwhelming psychological state on Judgment Day for those who wasted their lives.

Ayah 10-11 (Psychology of Denial and Cognitive Dissonance)

These ayahs brilliantly capture the psychology of denial by showing how the SAME WORDS carry opposite emotional content depending on psychological state:

In the worldly life (psychology of arrogance and denial): - The questions are rhetorical, dripping with sarcasm and superiority - They reflect cognitive closure - the mind is closed to possibilities that challenge existing beliefs - They demonstrate confirmation bias - seeking only evidence that supports preexisting disbelief - They show defensive intellectualization - using seemingly rational arguments to avoid uncomfortable truths

On Judgment Day (psychology of shock and regret): - The SAME questions now express disbelief at reality, not denial of possibility - They reflect cognitive dissonance - the clash between what they believed and what they’re experiencing - They demonstrate the collapse of defense mechanisms - no more rationalization, just raw shock - They show the impossibility of maintaining denial when confronted with undeniable reality

This captures a profound psychological truth: humans can hold contradictory beliefs, use words to mean opposite things, and shift from arrogance to humiliation when reality intrudes on denial.

The mention of “hollow bones” (عِظَامًا نَخِرَةً) also has psychological significance: it represents the ultimate symbol of death and decay. To say “even after we’re reduced to hollow bones” is to say “even after we’re utterly and completely gone.” The disbelievers are asserting that death is final, absolute, and irreversible. On Judgment Day, standing in resurrected bodies and still referencing those hollow bones will create maximal cognitive dissonance - “We WERE completely dead, completely gone, and yet here we are.”

Social Dynamics (Ayahs 1-5)

The raid imagery also reflects social and tribal dynamics of pre-Islamic Arabia:

  1. Tribal Warfare: Raids were part of the social structure - they redistributed wealth, established dominance, and maintained tribal honor systems.

  2. Social Coordination: Successful raids required social cohesion, trust, discipline, and clear hierarchies. The imagery of coordinated attack (الْمُدَبِّرَاتِ أَمْرًا) reflects values of planning and execution that Arabs prized.

  3. Vulnerability of Peace: The sleeping village represents the vulnerability of those who live in heedlessness. Socially, this was understood as a warning: never let your guard down.

By using this imagery for Judgment Day, Allah is saying: Just as vulnerable tribes who don’t prepare for attack will be devastated, vulnerable souls who don’t prepare for judgment will face devastation.

Political Implications

While this passage doesn’t directly address political structures, the concept of sudden accountability (Judgment Day as a raid) has political implications:

  1. All Power is Temporary: Just as raiders can strike any village regardless of its current strength, death and judgment can come to anyone regardless of worldly power.

  2. Ultimate Authority: The imagery establishes that there is an ultimate authority (Allah) before whom all worldly authorities must answer. This relativizes political power.

  3. Justice Will Be Established: For the oppressed and powerless, the message is hopeful - those who oppress them in this world will face judgment. For oppressors, it’s a warning.

Quranic Wisdom

The Nature of Divine Oaths (Ayahs 1-5)

Allah’s taking of oaths in the Quran serves multiple purposes of wisdom:

  1. Attention and Emphasis: Just as when a human swears, it signals “pay attention, this is important,” divine oaths serve to arrest our attention and prepare us for a crucial message.

  2. Invitation to Reflect: By swearing by created things (horses, winds, stars, time), Allah invites us to contemplate His creation. Each oath is an invitation to tadabbur - deep reflection on the signs (ayat) in creation.

  3. Connection Between Creator and Creation: The oaths connect revelation (the spoken word of Allah) with creation (the tangible works of Allah). This unifies our spiritual and empirical understanding - both revelation and creation are ayat (signs) from the same Divine source.

  4. Freedom from Rigid Formulas: The lesson from Dr. Bint al-Shati’s analysis is profound: Allah’s speech transcends human linguistic conventions. We cannot cage the Quran in rigid grammatical formulas. This teaches humility - our theories and frameworks must submit to the Word, not the other way around.

The Wisdom of Using Earthly Parallels for Otherworldly Realities (Ayahs 1-6)

Allah could have described Judgment Day directly, but instead He uses an earthly parallel (the raid) first. This contains multiple wisdoms:

  1. Making the Abstract Concrete: Judgment Day is beyond our experience, but a nighttime raid is something the original audience understood viscerally. By connecting the unknown to the known, Allah makes the abstract concrete and the distant immediate.

  2. Emotional Impact: A direct description of Judgment Day might remain intellectual. By first invoking the fear and shock of a raid - something people had experienced or feared - Allah accesses the emotional dimension, making the message penetrate the heart, not just the mind.

  3. Universal Application: While the specific imagery is of Arabian horse raids, the underlying psychological experience - sudden attack, terror, helplessness - is universal across cultures and times. Everyone can relate to the fear of sudden catastrophe.

  4. Sign Recognition: This teaches us to see the signs of the afterlife in this life. Just as the raid is a sign pointing to Judgment Day, all earthly experiences of justice, consequences, accountability are signs pointing to ultimate Justice, ultimate Consequence, ultimate Accountability.

The Earth as Witness and Agent (Ayah 6)

The profound wisdom in describing the earth itself as the agent of shaking (الرَّاجِفَةُ) includes:

  1. Creation Testifies: The earth has been witnessing human actions - good and evil - for all of history. On Judgment Day, it will testify: “That day, it will report its news” (99:4). The earth isn’t inert - it’s a witness that has been patiently observing and will eventually speak.

  2. Sin Affects Creation: The Quranic worldview holds that human sin impacts creation itself: “Corruption has appeared throughout the land and sea by what the hands of people have earned” (30:41). The earth wants to react to sin but Allah restrains it - this teaches that our actions have cosmic consequences, not just personal ones.

  3. No Escape: By making the earth itself the source of the shake, Allah teaches there’s no escape. You can’t flee from the earth - you’re standing on it. Similarly, there’s no escape from Judgment. Death comes to everyone, judgment comes to everyone.

  4. Allah’s Restraint is Mercy: The fact that Allah holds back the earth from reacting to sin is an act of mercy. He gives time, gives respite, gives opportunity to repent. But that restraint isn’t forever - there will come a day when He releases His hold.

The Connection Between Heart and Eyes (Ayahs 8-9)

The phrase “trembling hearts - their eyes humiliated” contains multiple layers of wisdom:

  1. Internal State Manifests Externally: You cannot hide what’s in your heart. Eventually, it shows in your face, your eyes, your demeanor. This is both a warning (your sins will be visible) and a comfort (the righteousness of believers will also be visible - their faces radiant, 80:38-39).

  2. Accountability is Personal: Each person’s eyes will show what their own heart contains. You can’t blame others, can’t hide behind the crowd. It’s individual accountability - your eyes tell your story, your heart’s story.

  3. Shame as Recognition of Truth: The humiliation described isn’t just fear of punishment - it’s the shame of recognition. Deep down, people always knew the truth. They suppressed it, denied it, mocked it. But when faced with it undeniably, shame floods in because they realize they always knew.

The Reversal of Words (Ayahs 10-11)

The wisdom in showing the same words said with opposite meaning is multilayered:

  1. Words Alone Are Insufficient: What matters isn’t just what you say but the heart with which you say it. The same words can be mockery or sincere shock. Allah judges hearts, not just speech.

  2. Mockery Returns: What you mock will confront you. The disbelievers mock resurrection using these words - “hollow bones in ditches, brought back? Impossible!” Those exact words will haunt them when they stand resurrected, made from those very hollow bones, pulled from those very ditches.

  3. Regret of Arrogance: The deepest regret on Judgment Day won’t just be “I was wrong” - it will be “I arrogantly mocked the truth, and now I’m living through what I ridiculed.” This teaches humility in this life - don’t mock what you don’t understand, don’t ridicule possibilities beyond your experience.

  4. Time for Reflection is Now: These ayahs are an invitation: those words can be said sarcastically now, or in terror later. Choose now how you want to say them - by taking the message seriously, preparing for judgment, and thus never having to say them in terror.

The Comprehensive Message

This passage teaches:

  1. Judgment is Certain: Whether you believe it or not, prepare for it or not, it’s coming. Denial doesn’t change reality.

  2. Judgment Will Be Sudden: Like a raid in the night, it will come when you don’t expect it. This isn’t to spread anxiety but to encourage constant readiness.

  3. Accountability is Individual: Your heart, your eyes, your words - they’re all yours. You’ll face the consequences of YOUR choices.

  4. Time is a Gift: The fact that judgment hasn’t happened yet is mercy - it’s time to change, time to prepare, time to turn to Allah.

  5. Humility is Essential: Approaching Allah’s word with humility - not arrogance, not mockery, but sincere desire to understand and submit - is the path to guidance.

  6. The Quran Connects to Reality: It’s not abstract theology - it uses real imagery, real human psychology, real experiences to communicate transcendent truths.

  7. Reflection is Worship: Thinking about the Quran, pondering its meanings, wondering about its wisdom - this isn’t academic exercise, it’s an act of worship, a way of building connection with Allah.

May Allah grant us the ability to reflect on His words with sincere hearts, to prepare for the Day we’ll meet Him, and to be among those whose hearts are at peace and whose faces are radiant on that Day. Allahumma Ameen.

Ayah 10–20 · linked video: 02. An-Nazi’at (Ayah 10-20)

Root Word Analysis

Ayah 10

يَقُولُونَ (yaquluna) [ق-و-ل]

The root ق-و-ل means to say or speak. The Form I verb قَالَ (qala) is the basic form meaning ‘he said.’ The present tense يَقُولُونَ (yaquluna) indicates a continuous or habitual action - ‘they are saying’ or ‘they say.’ This present tense can also express a future meaning in Quranic Arabic, suggesting this statement will be made on Judgment Day. The ongoing nature of the verb captures both the skeptics’ current statements and their future realization.

مَرْدُودُونَ (marduduna) [ر-د-د]

The root ر-د-د fundamentally means to return or go back to one’s origin. The passive participle مَرْدُودُونَ indicates ‘those who are returned’ or ‘brought back.’ The word راد in Arabic means rejection as well as return - so the skeptics are actually acknowledging they will return to their original state (dirt) while simultaneously rejecting this possibility. The verb رَدَّ also carries the meaning of going back to a starting point, which in this context is the original creation from earth.

الْحَافِرَةِ (al-hafirah) [ح-ف-ر]

The root ح-ف-ر means to dig or excavate. The noun حَافِرَة has multiple meanings derived from this root: (1) A ditch or grave, because graves are dug into the earth; (2) The original state or first condition. The word حَافِر refers to a horse’s hoof which digs into the ground, leaving an imprint. From this came the expression ‘standing in the hafira’ - remaining in one’s original position. The grave is called حَافِرَة because it is dug into the earth. Most significantly, حَافِرَة means the original state - returning to the first condition of creation.

Ayah 11

عِظَامًا (izhaman) [ع-ظ-م]

The root ع-ظ-م means greatness or magnitude, and عَظْم refers to bones - the great structural parts of the body. The plural عِظَام refers to multiple bones or the skeletal structure.

نَخِرَةً (nakhirah) [ن-خ-ر]

The root ن-خ-ر means to be hollow or decayed, with the specific meaning of producing sound through a hollow space. نَخِرَة describes bones that have become hollow and decayed. The word النَّخِير refers to a sound coming from a hollow place, like breathing through the nose (which is hollow inside - hence the nose is called مِنْخَر). The word specifically emphasizes the hollowness of decayed bones - they are empty shells. This is significant because humans are not entirely physical; there is a ruh (soul) inside, but the skeptics only see the hollow physical body.

Ayah 12

قَالُوا (qalu) [ق-و-ل]

The root ق-و-ل means to say. Here the past tense قَالُوا ‘they said’ creates a narrative shift. The ayah began with present tense يَقُولُونَ (they are saying), but now switches to past tense قَالُوا (they said). This creates a cinematic transition - taking the audience back in time to show these same people making their skeptical statements in the past, which they will now regret on Judgment Day.

كَرَّةٌ (karratun) [ك-ر-ر]

The root ك-ر-ر means to repeat or return. The word كَرَّة specifically means a return or a second time around. It implies resurrection - being brought back to life a second time. The word emphasizes repetition and coming back.

خَاسِرَةٌ (khasirah) [خ-س-ر]

The root خ-س-ر means loss or defeat. خَاسِرَة is an active participle meaning ‘losing’ or ‘full of loss.’ The form emphasizes the quality of being characterized by loss. The statement ‘that is a losing return’ is actually true - if resurrection happens and they disbelieved, it will indeed be a catastrophic loss for them. The Quran lets them speak a truth sarcastically, which will become their bitter reality.

Ayah 13

زَجْرَةٌ (zajrah) [ز-ج-ر]

The root ز-ج-ر means to scold, rebuke, or drive away harshly. The word زَجْرَة originally refers to scolding or shouting at animals to make them move or stop. When you yell at a horse or dog that misbehaves, that sharp command is called زَجْر. From this came the meaning of humiliating someone by speaking to them harshly, putting them in their place like one would an animal. Allah uses this word to describe the call of resurrection - it will be a single, sharp, commanding blast that requires no repetition. The word choice suggests the disbelievers will be silenced and humiliated, with no room for their previous arrogance.

وَاحِدَةٌ (wahidah) [و-ح-د]

The root و-ح-د means oneness or being singular. وَاحِدَة means ‘one’ or ‘single.’ The word emphasizes the unity and singularity of the event.

Ayah 14

السَّاهِرَةِ (as-sahirah) [س-ه-ر]

The root س-ه-ر means to stay awake at night or to be sleepless. السَّاهِرَة literally means ‘the sleepless night’ or ‘the night that prevents sleep.’ The word describes a night when one cannot sleep, typically due to fear, anxiety, or vigilance. The expression لَيْلَة سَاهِرَة (laylah sahirah) means a sleepless night. This connects powerfully to the raid imagery established earlier in the surah - when people are raided at night, they cannot sleep; they stand guard in terror, not knowing when or where the attack will come. The word also suggests darkness (night), indicating Judgment Day will not be bright - people will need light (nur) to see. The choice of this word paints a scene of anxious wakefulness, shock, and fear.

Ayah 15

حَدِيثُ (hadithu) [ح-د-ث]

The root ح-د-ث means something new that happens or occurs. حَدِيث means news, story, or narration - information about something that has occurred. The word emphasizes that this is newsworthy information, a significant account.

مُوسَىٰ (Musa)

The name of the Prophet Musa (AS) - Moses. This is not derived from Arabic roots but is the Arabic rendering of his name.

أَتَاكَ (ataka) [أ-ت-ي]

The root أ-ت-ي means to come or arrive. The Form I perfect verb أَتَىٰ with the attached pronoun كَ creates أَتَاكَ meaning ‘it came to you’ or ‘it reached you.’ The verb emphasizes the transmission of information.

Ayah 16

نَادَاهُ (nadahu) [ن-د-ي]

The root ن-د-ي means to call out to someone. The Form III verb نَادَىٰ emphasizes calling from a distance or calling with announcement. نَادَاهُ means ‘He called him’ - Allah called Musa (AS).

رَبُّهُ (rabbuhu) [ر-ب-ب]

The root ر-ب-ب means to nurture, sustain, maintain, and have mastery over. The noun رَبّ means Lord, Master, Sustainer, Nurturer. With the attached pronoun هُ it becomes رَبُّهُ ‘his Lord’ or ‘his Master.’ The word emphasizes Allah’s role as the One who nurtures, sustains, and has authority.

الْوَادِ (al-wadi) [و-د-ي]

The root و-د-ي refers to a valley or low-lying area between mountains. الْوَادِ means ‘the valley.’

الْمُقَدَّسِ (al-muqaddas) [ق-د-س]

The root ق-د-س means purity, holiness, sanctity. The Form II passive participle مُقَدَّس means ‘sanctified’ or ‘made sacred.’ The intensive Form II suggests something purified and made extremely holy. الْمُقَدَّس means ‘the sacred’ or ‘the sanctified.’

طُوًى (Tuwa)

This is the name of the sacred valley, though there are multiple interpretations of its meaning. Some scholars say it is simply the proper name of the location (Mount Sinai area). Others derive linguistic meanings: from the root ط-و-ي meaning to fold or roll up - suggesting either (1) a folded portion of the night when this occurred, (2) the folding/closing of distance between the heavens and earth when Allah spoke to Musa (AS), or (3) a valley that was only sacred during that folded moment of time when Allah’s speech occurred there.

Ayah 17

اذْهَبْ (idh-hab) [ذ-ه-ب]

The root ذ-ه-ب means to go or depart. The imperative form اذْهَبْ is a command: ‘Go!’ This is a direct order from Allah to Musa (AS).

فِرْعَوْنَ (fir’awn)

This is not an Arabic word but the Quranic rendering of ‘Pharaoh’ from ancient Egyptian. The original pronunciation is suggested to be ‘Per-aa’ or ‘Para’ meaning ‘the Great House.’ The Pharaohs were symbolized by the massive monuments and castles they built - they weren’t just people but were identified with these colossal structures. This instilled fear in enemies and subjects alike.

طَغَىٰ (tagha) [ط-غ-ي]

The root ط-غ-ي means to transgress, exceed bounds, rebel, or overflow. The verb طَغَىٰ means ‘he has transgressed’ or ‘he has rebelled.’ Like water that overflows its banks (طُغْيَان الْمَاء), Pharaoh has exceeded all limits - he doesn’t care what laws he breaks, what crimes he commits, or whom he kills. The word captures his complete disregard for any moral or divine boundaries.

Ayah 18

هَل (hal)

An interrogative particle meaning ‘is there’ or ‘would you’ - used to ask questions, often with a sense of offering or suggesting something politely.

لَّكَ (laka)

The preposition لِ (for/to) combined with the pronoun كَ (you) creates لَكَ meaning ‘for you’ or ‘for yourself.’

أَن (an)

A particle that introduces a verbal clause, often translated as ‘that’ or ‘to.’

تَزَكَّىٰ (tazakka) [ز-ك-ي]

The root ز-ك-ي means purity, growth, increase, and blessing. The Form V verb تَزَكَّىٰ is reflexive, meaning to purify oneself, to seek purification and growth, to become better. The word doesn’t just mean ritual purity but encompasses spiritual growth, moral improvement, and increasing in goodness and blessings. The question ‘Would you consider that you might purify yourself?’ is remarkably gentle - it’s not demanding immediate transformation but suggesting a possible journey toward betterment.

Ayah 19

أَهْدِيَكَ (ahdiyaka) [ه-د-ي]

The root ه-د-ي means to guide, show the way, or lead someone on the right path. The Form I verb هَدَىٰ means ‘he guided.’ The form أَهْدِيَكَ is first-person imperfect with attached pronoun: ‘I guide you’ or ‘I may guide you.’ The verb emphasizes showing someone the correct path and leading them to their destination.

رَبِّكَ (rabbika) [ر-ب-ب]

The root ر-ب-ب means to nurture, sustain, and have mastery over. رَبّ means Lord, Master, Sustainer. With the attached pronoun كَ it becomes رَبِّكَ ‘your Lord.’ Even when addressing Pharaoh who claimed divinity, Musa (AS) speaks of ‘your Lord’ - acknowledging that Pharaoh has a Master above him.

فَتَخْشَىٰ (fatakhsha) [خ-ش-ي]

The root خ-ش-ي means to fear with reverence, to be in awe of, to have consciousness of. The verb تَخْشَىٰ means ‘you fear’ or ‘you become fearful.’ With the particle فَ (so/then), فَتَخْشَىٰ means ‘so that you may fear’ or ‘perhaps you will fear.’ This is not fear of physical harm but reverential fear (taqwa) - consciousness and awe of Allah. The statement suggests that even Pharaoh could potentially develop this quality.

Ayah 20

فَأَرَاهُ (fa-arahu) [ر-أ-ي]

The root ر-أ-ي means to see or show. The Form IV verb أَرَىٰ means ‘he showed.’ The form فَأَرَاهُ means ‘so he showed him’ - Musa (AS) showed Pharaoh something.

الْآيَةَ (al-ayah) [أ-ي-ي]

The root أ-ي-ي relates to signs or miracles. الْآيَة means ‘the sign’ or ‘the miracle’ - a proof or evidence that demonstrates truth.

الْكُبْرَىٰ (al-kubra) [ك-ب-ر]

The root ك-ب-ر means greatness or magnitude. الْكُبْرَىٰ is the feminine superlative form meaning ‘the greatest’ or ‘the supreme.’ الْآيَةَ الْكُبْرَىٰ means ‘the greatest sign.’ The Quran deliberately doesn’t specify which miracle this was (the staff, the hand, the plagues) - leaving it general to emphasize its overwhelming nature rather than getting caught in details.

Grammatical & Rhetorical Nuances

Ayah 10

The verb يَقُولُونَ (yaquluna) is in the present tense but carries dual temporal meaning - it describes what skeptics currently say and what will be said on Judgment Day. This ambiguity is intentional, creating a bridge between present disbelief and future realization.

The passive participle مَرْدُودُونَ (returned ones) places the skeptics in a passive role - they are being acted upon, returned against their will, which contradicts their skeptical confidence. The question form أَئِنَّا (are we really?) expresses incredulity and sarcasm.

The phrase فِي الْحَافِرَةِ carries a brilliant double meaning through the single word الْحَافِرَة - simultaneously meaning ‘into the ditch/grave’ and ‘into the original state.’ This dual semantic loading is a rhetorical device called تَوْرِية (deliberate ambiguity) where one word carries multiple complementary meanings. The skeptics are questioning both their return to the grave (physical) and return to their original state of creation (metaphysical).

Ayah 11

The phrase أَئِذَا كُنَّا عِظَامًا نَخِرَةً uses the perfect tense كُنَّا (we were/became) to emphasize the completion of decay - ‘when we have become decayed bones.’ The adjective نَخِرَة (hollow/decayed) follows the noun عِظَامًا for emphasis, stressing the complete deterioration.

The word نَخِرَة specifically emphasizes hollowness, which is rhetorically significant - it reveals the skeptics’ materialistic worldview. They see only the hollow physical body (bones) and cannot conceive of the ruh (soul) within. This linguistic choice subtly critiques their limited vision.

Ayah 12

The shift from present tense يَقُولُونَ (they are saying) in Ayah 10 to past tense قَالُوا (they said) in Ayah 12 creates a cinematic flashback effect. The Quran is showing the same people at two different moments - their past sarcastic statement and their future regret. This temporal shift is a powerful rhetorical device that creates narrative depth.

The phrase تِلْكَ إِذًا كَرَّةٌ خَاسِرَةٌ uses تِلْكَ (that) as a demonstrative pointing to something distant or hypothetical - the resurrection they’re mocking. إِذًا (then/in that case) adds conditional emphasis. The word order places كَرَّةٌ (return) before its modifier خَاسِرَةٌ (losing), with the indefinite forms creating emphasis through tanwin (nunation).

The statement is deeply ironic - they say it sarcastically (‘Oh that would be such a terrible return - NOT!’), but it’s literally true. If resurrection happens and they disbelieved, it will be catastrophically bad. The Quran preserves their sarcastic statement, which will haunt them when it becomes reality. This is rhetorical irony (مُفَارَقَة) at its finest.

Ayah 13

The word فَإِنَّمَا begins with فَ (then/so) indicating consequence, and إِنَّمَا is a restriction particle meaning ‘it is only’ or ‘it is merely.’ This combination emphasizes the ease and simplicity of resurrection - it’s ONLY one blast, nothing more needed.

The phrase هِيَ زَجْرَةٌ وَاحِدَةٌ uses the pronoun هِيَ (it) to emphasize the subject before stating it. The word زَجْرَةٌ (scolding/blast) is indefinite for emphasis, and وَاحِدَةٌ (single/one) is placed after as a powerful modifier.

The addition of tā’ marbūṭah (ة) in وَاحِدَةٌ creates what’s called تَاءُ الْمَرَّة (tā’ of single occurrence) - a grammatical marker indicating something happens exactly once and never needs repetition. This isn’t just ‘one blast’ but ‘one single definitive blast that will never need to be repeated.’ The grammar itself emphasizes finality.

The choice of زَجْرَة (a word used for scolding animals) over other words for ‘call’ or ‘blast’ is rhetorically brilliant - it suggests the disbelievers will be humiliated, silenced, and put in their place like misbehaving animals. Their previous arrogance will be completely crushed.

Ayah 14

The phrase فَإِذَا هُم بِالسَّاهِرَةِ uses فَإِذَا (then suddenly) to indicate immediate consequence - no delay between the blast and their presence in the Sahirah. The structure إِذَا + pronoun + بِ creates emphasis on the shock and suddenness.

The definite article in السَّاهِرَةِ (the Sahirah) suggests a known or specific location/state, though it’s defined only by this Quranic usage. The word’s root meaning (sleepless night) is rhetorically loaded with multiple implications simultaneously: darkness, terror, inability to rest, anxious vigilance.

This word choice creates powerful coherence with the raid imagery from earlier in the surah. Just as raided people cannot sleep (standing guard in terror), those on Judgment Day will find themselves in a ‘sleepless night’ - the metaphor completes the painting begun with the oath about raiders.

Ayah 15

The question هَلْ أَتَاكَ حَدِيثُ مُوسَىٰ uses the interrogative particle هَلْ which doesn’t expect a ‘no’ answer - it’s rhetorical, assuming the Prophet ﷺ already knows this story. This is called اِسْتِفْهَامٌ تَقْرِيرِيّ (confirmatory questioning) used to draw attention and emphasize rather than to seek information.

The word حَدِيث (news/story) rather than قِصَّة (story/tale) emphasizes the freshness and relevance - ‘Think of this as new news just reaching you now, with immediate relevance to your situation.’

The placement of this question immediately after the Judgment Day description creates a powerful transition - just when the audience is overwhelmed by the resurrection scene, the Quran pivots to a historical parallel. This rhetorical technique is called اِنْتِقَال (transition) and serves to connect abstract eschatological concepts with concrete historical examples.

Ayah 16

The phrase إِذْ نَادَاهُ رَبُّهُ uses إِذْ (when) to mark a specific moment in time. The verb نَادَىٰ (called) is Form III, suggesting calling from a distance or with gravitas.

The subject رَبُّهُ (his Lord) is placed after the verb for emphasis - the One calling is not just anyone but his Master and Sustainer. The attached pronoun هُ (his) personalizes the relationship even when speaking about Allah.

The prepositional phrase بِالْوَادِ الْمُقَدَّسِ طُوًى is rich with meaning. The بِ (in/at) indicates location. The word الْوَادِ (the valley) is definite, suggesting a specific known place. الْمُقَدَّسِ (the sacred) is an intensive Form II passive participle - this valley was made extremely sacred.

The word طُوًى at the end serves as بَدَل (apposition) - explaining which valley is meant. Some scholars read it as an adjective meaning ‘folded,’ creating the interpretation that Allah ‘folded’ the distance between heaven and earth during this encounter - a beautiful metaphorical reading.

Ayah 17

The imperative اذْهَبْ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ (Go to Pharaoh) is direct and unambiguous - a clear command. The use of إِلَىٰ (to/toward) rather than لِ emphasizes directed movement toward a specific target.

The phrase إِنَّهُ طَغَىٰ uses إِنَّ for emphasis and certainty - ‘Indeed, verily, surely he has transgressed.’ This is اِسْتِئْنَاف (independent clause) giving the reason for the command. The perfect tense طَغَىٰ indicates a completed action whose effects continue - his transgression is established fact.

The brevity of this ayah (only 5 words in Arabic) creates impact through conciseness - the command is simple, the reason is clear, action must be taken. This rhetorical minimalism (إِيجَاز) is more powerful than lengthy explanation.

Ayah 18

The question فَقُلْ هَل لَّكَ إِلَىٰ أَن تَزَكَّىٰ begins with imperative فَقُلْ (so say) commanding Musa (AS) on what exactly to say to Pharaoh. The هَلْ introduces a gentle, polite question - not a demand or accusation.

The phrase لَّكَ إِلَىٰ literally means ‘for you toward’ which creates the sense of ‘Would you [consider] for yourself [heading] toward…?’ The preposition إِلَىٰ (toward) suggests movement and direction - not demanding arrival but suggesting a path to consider.

The verb تَزَكَّىٰ (purify yourself/grow) is Form V reflexive - the action must be done by the subject himself. This is not ‘Can I purify you?’ but ‘Would you consider purifying yourself?’ - acknowledging Pharaoh’s agency and free will.

The entire phrase is remarkably gentle and respectful. Despite Pharaoh’s crimes, Allah instructs Musa (AS) to approach him with an invitation, not a threat. This demonstrates the principle that da’wah begins with mercy and respect, not with confrontation.

Ayah 19

The phrase وَأَهْدِيَكَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ continues the gentle tone with وَ (and) connecting to the previous offer. The verb أَهْدِيَكَ (I may guide you) is in a form that can express possibility/permission rather than definite future - ‘Perhaps I might guide you’ rather than ‘I will definitely guide you.’

The phrase إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ (toward your Lord) is profound - even when addressing Pharaoh who claimed to be god, Musa (AS) speaks of ‘your Lord,’ linguistically asserting that Pharaoh has a Master above him. The attached pronoun كَ (your) personalizes it - this concerns YOUR Lord specifically.

The structure ‘perhaps I may guide you toward your Lord’ is less direct than ‘I will guide you to Allah’ - it’s an offer of possibility, respecting Pharaoh’s choice while planting the seed that guidance is available if he wants it.

The phrase فَتَخْشَىٰ (so that you may fear/become conscious) uses فَ indicating purpose or result. The verb is in a form suggesting possibility - ‘so that perhaps you might develop consciousness of Him.’ The entire verse progression moves from purification (internal growth) to guidance (direction toward Allah) to consciousness (the fruit of that journey).

Ayah 20

The phrase فَأَرَاهُ الْآيَةَ الْكُبْرَىٰ uses فَ to indicate sequence - after giving the message, then he showed the sign. The verb أَرَىٰ is Form IV causative meaning ‘he showed/made him see.’

The object الْآيَةَ الْكُبْرَىٰ (the greatest sign) is definite with the article, indicating a specific known sign. الْكُبْرَىٰ is the superlative feminine form - ‘the greatest’ or ‘the supreme.’

Rhetorically powerful is what’s NOT said - the Quran doesn’t specify which sign (the staff transforming, the hand becoming white, the plagues). This omission is deliberate (حَذْف) - the focus is not on identifying the specific miracle but on emphasizing its overwhelming greatness. Providing specifics would invite debate about which miracle was greatest; leaving it general maintains focus on the response. This is eloquent restraint.

Historical & Biblical Context

Ayahs 10-12

These ayahs capture the skeptical statements of the Makkan disbelievers regarding resurrection. During the Makkan period, the Quraysh repeatedly mocked the concept of bodily resurrection after death. They would sarcastically ask, ‘Are we really going to be brought back after we’ve turned to dust and bones?’ This incredulity is documented throughout the Makkan surahs. The sarcastic tone in their statement ‘That would be a losing return’ reflects their complete dismissal of accountability in the afterlife.

The historical context shows that the Quraysh’s rejection wasn’t merely theological - it was tied to their economic and political interests. Accepting resurrection and judgment would mean accepting moral accountability, which threatened their established order based on tribal loyalty and material wealth rather than divine law.

Ayahs 15-20

These ayahs reference the story of Prophet Musa (AS) and Pharaoh, one of the most detailed narratives in the Quran. The ‘sacred valley of Tuwa’ (al-wadi al-muqaddas Tuwa) refers to the location on Mount Sinai where Allah first spoke to Musa (AS) and commanded him to remove his sandals because he was on sacred ground (as detailed in Surah Ta-Ha 20:12).

The historical Pharaoh that Musa (AS) confronted is believed by many scholars to be Ramses II, though the Quran doesn’t specify. The title ‘Pharaoh’ (Fir’awn) comes from the ancient Egyptian ‘Per-aa’ meaning ‘Great House,’ referring to the royal palace. Over time, this became a title for the king himself. The Pharaohs built massive monuments and structures partly to instill fear in enemies and subjects, making themselves appear as god-like figures whose power was eternal.

The economic system of ancient Egypt relied heavily on slave labor, particularly from the Israelites. When Musa (AS) came with the message to free the Israelites, it wasn’t just a religious challenge but an economic and political threat to Pharaoh’s entire system. The Pharaohs had created a religious ideology where they were considered divine or semi-divine descendants of the sun god Ra, making rebellion against them not just political treason but religious sacrilege.

The ‘greatest sign’ (al-ayah al-kubra) mentioned in Ayah 20 likely refers to one of the major miracles shown to Pharaoh - either Musa’s staff transforming into a serpent, his hand becoming radiant white, or the plagues that followed. The biblical account in Exodus confirms these miracles, though with some differences in detail.

Connection to Biblical Narrative

The Quranic account parallels the biblical Exodus narrative but with distinct emphases. Both scriptures record: - Allah’s call to Musa (AS) at the sacred location (Mount Sinai) - The command to go to Pharaoh - The performance of miracles as signs - Pharaoh’s rejection and arrogance

However, the Quran emphasizes Pharaoh’s spiritual opportunity - the remarkable gentleness in the initial invitation (‘Would you consider purifying yourself?’) is more pronounced in the Quranic account than in Exodus, where the emphasis is more on the demand to ‘let my people go.’ The Quran stresses that even Pharaoh, despite his immense crimes, was given a respectful invitation to guidance, highlighting Allah’s mercy and the human capacity for change.

The Quran mentions that Musa (AS) was sent with his brother Harun (Aaron) to Pharaoh with ‘gentle speech’ (qawlan layyinan) as recorded in Surah Ta-Ha (20:44), though Harun is not mentioned in this brief Surah An-Nazi’at account.

Relevance to the Prophet’s ﷺ Context

The story of Musa (AS) and Pharaoh is directly superimposed over the Prophet Muhammad’s ﷺ situation with the Quraysh. Just as Pharaoh was the most powerful ruler of his time with seemingly absolute authority, the Quraysh held power in Makkah through their control of the Ka’bah and trade routes. Just as Pharaoh built a religious-political system to maintain power, the Quraysh maintained 360 idols at the Ka’bah representing different tribes, creating a religious tourism economy.

When the Prophet ﷺ called to pure monotheism and the destruction of idols, it threatened the Quraysh’s entire economic and political structure, just as Musa’s call threatened Pharaoh’s. The parallel would not be lost on the Makkan audience - if Allah destroyed the mighty Pharaoh for rejecting His messenger, what hope did the relatively weaker Quraysh have?

Coherence

The passage from Ayah 10 to Ayah 20 demonstrates exquisite thematic and structural coherence within Surah An-Nazi’at and the Quran’s broader narrative strategy.

Connection to Previous Passage

The surah opened with powerful oaths depicting raiders who snatch violently and suddenly (ayahs 1-5), followed by the description of Judgment Day’s earthquakes and terror (ayahs 6-9). Ayahs 10-14 continue this theme by presenting the skeptics’ dismissal of resurrection, followed immediately by Allah’s response that it will take only ‘one blast’ and they’ll find themselves in the Sahirah (the terrifying sleepless night).

The word choice of الْسَّاهِرَة (the sleepless night) creates brilliant coherence with the raid imagery from the opening oaths. When people are raided at night while sleeping, they cannot return to sleep - they remain vigilant in terror, not knowing when the next attack will come. Similarly, on Judgment Day, the criminals will find themselves in a state of terrified wakefulness, unable to rest, awaiting divine judgment. The painting begun with the opening oaths is completed here.

Transition to the Musa Narrative (Ayahs 15-20)

The sudden shift from Judgment Day to the story of Musa (AS) might seem abrupt, but it serves multiple coherence functions:

Thematic Parallel: The entire surah is about two forms of divine raids/attacks: 1. The eschatological raid - angels descending on Judgment Day to judge humanity 2. The historical raid - Allah’s punishment of nations that rejected messengers

Just as Allah will send legions of angels to judge on the Last Day, He sent Musa (AS) to warn Pharaoh before sending destruction upon him. The message is: Judgment is coming through Allah’s armies (angels), but first Allah sends messengers mercifully, without armies, giving people a chance to respond.

Prophetic Parallel: The story of Musa (AS) confronting Pharaoh directly mirrors the Prophet Muhammad’s ﷺ situation with Quraysh: - Both faced powerful establishments (Pharaoh vs. Quraysh) - Both had religious-economic systems to challenge (Egyptian state religion vs. Ka’bah idol system) - Both came with messages that threatened political power - Both were minorities speaking truth to power

The Makkan audience would understand: Just as Musa (AS) was sent without an army but with truth, our Prophet ﷺ comes without force - but don’t be deceived by the absence of physical armies. The armies of Allah are there in the unseen, ready to support truth.

Mercy Before Judgment: The gentle language Allah commanded Musa (AS) to use (‘Would you consider purifying yourself?’) demonstrates that even to the worst tyrant in history, Allah’s approach through His messenger was one of mercy, respect, and opportunity for change. This establishes the principle that da’wah begins with kindness, not confrontation. Only when the pharaoh rejected this gentle invitation did punishment come.

This connects back to the Judgment Day imagery - the terror described in ayahs 6-14 is what awaits those who reject the merciful invitation given in this life. The two parts (judgment description + Musa story) work together: here’s what’s coming, but here’s also how Allah gives chances through messengers first.

Connection to Surah’s Conclusion

Ayah 26 (coming later in the surah) states ‘In that is surely a lesson for those who fear.’ The Musa-Pharaoh narrative serves as the concrete historical example of the abstract eschatological principles. Pharaoh was made an example (نَكَالًا) for both later and earlier generations - demonstrating that Allah’s promise of judgment is not empty but has historical precedent.

Connection to Quran’s Musa Narratives

Musa (AS) is mentioned more than any other prophet in the Quran (approximately 136 times). His story is told in varying detail across different surahs, each time emphasizing aspects relevant to that surah’s themes:

  • Surah Al-Baqarah focuses on the Israelites’ rebellion
  • Surah Ta-Ha provides the most detailed account of Musa’s call and confrontation with Pharaoh
  • Surah Al-Qasas narrates Musa’s early life and flight to Midian
  • Here in Surah An-Nazi’at, the focus is extremely concise - only the essential elements needed to make the point about messengers being sent before judgment

The brevity in this surah is deliberate - lengthy details would distract from the core message: Powerful tyrants have been warned by seemingly powerless messengers, yet when they rejected, they were destroyed. Take warning.

Rhetorical Progression

The passage moves from: 1. Skeptics’ mocking question (10-11) 2. Their sarcastic dismissal (12) 3. Allah’s response - it only takes one blast (13) 4. The result - they’re in the terrifying Sahirah (14) 5. Transition: Remember Musa’s story (15) 6. Musa’s gentle invitation to the worst tyrant (18-19) 7. Showing him clear signs (20)

This progression moves from mockery → divine response → historical precedent → methodology of da’wah. The structure teaches: Don’t be disturbed by mockery; Allah’s response is coming; history shows how He deals with rejecters; and the correct methodology is always mercy and gentleness first.

Psychological, Political & Social

Psychological Insights (Ayahs 10-12)

Cognitive Dissonance of Skepticism: The skeptics’ question ‘Are we really going to be returned to our original state after we’ve become hollow bones?’ reveals a psychological defense mechanism. They can only conceive of the physical body (hollow bones) and cannot comprehend the spiritual dimension (the ruh/soul within). This materialistic worldview is a form of psychological limitation - they’ve confined reality to what they can physically observe, closing their minds to the unseen realm.

The sarcastic statement ‘That would be a losing return’ (Ayah 12) demonstrates the psychology of dismissive mockery. When people feel threatened by an idea that challenges their worldview, they often resort to sarcasm as a defense mechanism. By mocking the concept, they avoid genuinely engaging with its implications. This sarcasm will later become their bitter regret when they realize the truth of what they mocked.

Temporal Perception: The Quran’s shift from present tense (يَقُولُونَ - they are saying) to past tense (قَالُوا - they said) creates a psychological effect of inevitability. It’s as if their future regret is already a past event - emphasizing that their denial won’t change the reality of what’s coming. This linguistic technique creates psychological pressure by treating their future accountability as an already-established fact.

Social Dynamics

Collective Denial: The plural form ‘they say’ indicates this is not individual skepticism but collective social phenomenon. When entire communities adopt skeptical attitudes toward resurrection and accountability, it becomes socially reinforced. People take comfort in numbers - ‘Everyone else thinks this way too, so we must be right.’ This groupthink mentality prevents individuals from critically examining beliefs.

The Power of Sarcasm in Social Control: The mocking tone ‘Oh that would be such a terrible return!’ serves a social function - it discourages others from taking the message seriously. When influential people in a society mock an idea, it becomes socially unacceptable to believe it. This is a form of social pressure and control, where ridicule is weaponized to maintain the status quo.

Political Analysis (Ayahs 17-20)

Religion as Political Tool: The Pharaoh’s claim to divinity (‘I am your supreme lord’ mentioned elsewhere) was not mere psychological delusion - it was calculated political strategy. By creating a religious framework where he was god or god’s representative, Pharaoh made rebellion against him not just political treason but religious blasphemy. This is the ultimate form of political control - fusing religious authority with political power so that challenging one is challenging both.

The massive monuments and pyramids Pharaoh built served political purposes: they instilled fear in enemies, demonstrated power to subjects, and created the visual impression of god-like capability (‘Only gods could build such structures’). Architecture was propaganda. The buildings weren’t just houses - they were political statements of eternal power.

Economic Foundations of Tyranny: Pharaoh’s system depended on slave labor (the Israelites). Free labor allowed him to build those monuments and maintain military power. When Musa (AS) demanded the Israelites be freed, it wasn’t just a moral request - it was an economic threat to the entire Egyptian system. Pharaoh’s refusal was economically motivated. This reveals how oppression is often sustained by economic interests - those benefiting from unjust systems resist change because liberation threatens their wealth and power.

Parallel to Makkan Politics: The Quraysh’s opposition to the Prophet ﷺ was similarly economic and political. The 360 idols at the Ka’bah represented different tribes - each idol was effectively a ‘customer base’ bringing pilgrims who spent money in Makkah. The Quraysh controlled this religious tourism economy. When the Prophet ﷺ called for idol destruction and pure monotheism, the Quraysh saw their economic empire threatened. Their religious arguments were often covers for economic fears.

This pattern repeats throughout history: when religion is intertwined with economics and politics, challenging religious corruption becomes extremely dangerous because powerful interests fight back. The Quran exposes this pattern by showing it in Pharaoh’s Egypt and implicitly in Makkan society.

Psychological Aspects of Da’wah (Ayahs 18-19)

Gentle Approach to the Hardened Heart: Despite Pharaoh being history’s worst tyrant, Allah instructed Musa (AS) to approach him with remarkable gentleness: ‘Would you consider that you might purify yourself? Perhaps I can guide you toward your Lord so you may become conscious of Him.’

This teaches profound psychological wisdom about human change:

  1. Agency and Choice: The question format (‘Would you consider?’) respects Pharaoh’s free will. Even confronting a tyrant, the approach isn’t ‘You MUST change’ but ‘Would you like to consider changing?’ This acknowledges that genuine transformation must be chosen, not coerced.

  2. Focus on Benefits: The invitation emphasizes what Pharaoh would gain (‘purify yourself’ - become better, grow, find blessings) rather than what he must give up. Psychologically, people are more motivated by potential gains than by fear of loss. Starting with ‘here’s how you can improve your life’ is more effective than ‘here’s what’s wrong with you.’

  3.
