Incremental Invitation: The progression is gentle - ‘consider purifying yourself’ → ‘I may guide you’ → ‘you might become conscious of Allah.’ Each step is presented as a possibility to explore, not a demand to fulfill immediately. This psychological approach meets people where they are and invites them on a journey rather than demanding they teleport to the destination.

  4. Humanizing the Enemy: By approaching even Pharaoh with respect and gentle speech, Allah demonstrates that we don’t dehumanize opponents. Every human being, no matter how far they’ve fallen, retains human dignity and the potential for change. This prevents the psychology of ‘us vs. them’ that leads to hatred and violence.

Social Implications

Minority Speaking Truth to Power: Both Musa (AS) and Muhammad ﷺ were minorities confronting powerful establishments. This establishes a pattern for believers - being a minority doesn’t invalidate your message. Truth isn’t determined by majority opinion or institutional power. Social validation (what ‘everyone thinks’) is not the metric for truth.

The Role of Messengers in Society: Messengers come without armies, without political power, without economic leverage - they come only with a message. This challenges the worldly assumption that power and authority require force and wealth. The Prophet ﷺ had no army in Makkah, yet he was speaking the most important truth. This teaches that moral authority doesn’t depend on material power.

The Sahaba understood this parallel - when first commanded to fight, they said they wouldn’t be like the Israelites who refused to fight alongside Musa (AS). They recognized their social role in relation to the Prophet ﷺ was analogous to the Israelites’ role with Musa (AS).

Human Nature and Accountability

The Psychology of Denial: The skeptics’ dismissal of resurrection reveals a deep human tendency - avoiding thoughts of accountability. If there’s no afterlife, no judgment, then there are no ultimate consequences for actions. This is psychologically comfortable for those living unjustly. Accepting resurrection means accepting that all actions have meaning and consequences - a psychologically heavier burden but also a more meaningful existence.

Selective Vision: The focus on ‘hollow bones’ shows how people selectively perceive reality to match their beliefs. They see the physical decay but refuse to consider the non-physical soul. This selective vision is a common human pattern - we see what confirms our existing beliefs and filter out what challenges them.

The Weight of Words: The Quran preserving their sarcastic statement (‘That would be a losing return!’) to confront them with it later teaches that words have weight and consequences. What we say casually, mockingly, or sarcastically may come back to haunt us. This should make us more conscious of our speech.

Quranic Wisdom

The Reality of Resurrection (Ayahs 10-14)

Divine Simplicity vs. Human Skepticism: Humanity questions and mocks the possibility of resurrection, seeing it as impossibly complex (‘How can scattered bones be reassembled?’). Yet Allah responds that it requires only ‘one single scolding’ (zajrah wahidah) - one blast, one command, and it’s done. This teaches profound wisdom: What seems impossible to creation is effortless for the Creator. Human limitations don’t define divine capability. We judge possibility by our own limited power, forgetting that Allah’s power is limitless.

The phrase ‘one single scolding that will never need repetition’ teaches that Allah’s commands don’t require multiple attempts or adjustments. He doesn’t need to ‘try again’ or ‘fix mistakes.’ When He commands ‘Be!’ it is - perfectly, completely, the first time. This should increase our trust in Allah’s promises: if He says resurrection will happen, it will happen exactly as He described, without fail.

The Night of Awakening: Finding themselves suddenly in ‘the Sahirah’ (the sleepless night) teaches that Judgment Day is not rest or peace for the guilty - it’s anxious wakefulness, anticipation of judgment, inability to escape awareness. Those who ‘slept’ through their moral responsibilities in this life (ignored the truth, suppressed their conscience) will be forced into consciousness on that Day. There will be no escape from awareness, no comfortable denial. This teaches that moral consciousness avoided in this life will be unavoidable in the next.

The Mercy in Warning (Ayahs 15-20)

Allah’s Methodology: Messengers Before Judgment: The transition from describing Judgment Day to narrating Musa’s story teaches a fundamental divine principle: Allah’s mercy always precedes His justice. Before sending armies of angels to judge and punish on the Last Day, He sent prophets and messengers to every nation with warnings and guidance. No one will be punished without first receiving clear warning and opportunity to change.

This teaches believers to never despair of anyone’s potential for guidance - if Allah sent Musa (AS) to Pharaoh with a gentle invitation, who are we to write anyone off as ‘too far gone’? Every human being, until their last breath, has the potential for transformation.

The Gentleness of Truth: The most striking wisdom in this passage is Allah’s instruction to Musa (AS): speak to the baby-killing, oppressive, self-declared god Pharaoh with the gentle question ‘Would you consider purifying yourself?’ This teaches:

  1. Truth doesn’t need force: Real truth is inherently powerful. It doesn’t require aggressive delivery or harsh words to be effective. The message itself carries weight; the delivery should carry wisdom and mercy.

  2. Respect the humanity in everyone: Even addressing history’s worst tyrant, the approach is respectful of his agency and potential. This prevents us from dehumanizing opponents. Everyone is a human being with a soul, created by Allah, with the capacity for change.

  3. Start with benefits, not threats: The invitation begins with ‘Would you like to become better? Grow? Find purification and guidance?’ rather than ‘Believe or you’ll be destroyed!’ Psychologically and spiritually, humans respond better to hope than to fear, to potential growth than to threats. The call to Islam should sound attractive - an invitation to goodness, not just an escape from punishment.

  4. Even harsh truths can be delivered gently: Later, Musa (AS) did warn Pharaoh of consequences, but the initial approach was gentle. This teaches balance - we don’t compromise truth, but we deliver it with wisdom and kindness.

Life Lessons: The Prophet’s Mission and Ours

Understanding Our Historical Moment: Just as Musa (AS) confronted Pharaoh and the Prophet ﷺ confronted Quraysh, Muslims today face environments that may be hostile to Islamic values. This is not a deviation from the norm - it IS the norm. Messengers and their followers have always been minorities speaking truth in challenging environments. Rather than complain about society’s condition, we should recognize this is exactly the environment in which believers are meant to shine.

You Don’t Need an Army to Speak Truth: Messengers came without political power, military force, or economic leverage. They came with truth. This teaches that moral authority doesn’t depend on material power. Today’s Muslims don’t need to wait until they have political control or social acceptance to live and speak Islamic values. Truth carries its own authority.

The Quraysh vs. Pharaoh: If Allah could destroy Pharaoh - the mightiest king with seemingly absolute power - what real power did the Quraysh have? They controlled mud homes in the middle of a desert, surrounded by empires that barely noticed them. Yet they thought they were invincible. This teaches: worldly power is illusory. What seems mighty today can be dust tomorrow. Only Allah’s power is real and lasting. This should liberate believers from being impressed or intimidated by worldly powers.

Don’t Run from Society: Some Muslims think the solution is to hide from society in ‘Islamic bubbles’ - Islamic schools, Islamic neighborhoods, Islamic everything, avoiding all fitna (trials). But messengers were sent INTO corrupt societies. Light is only useful where there’s darkness. The Ummah’s purpose is to be in society, bringing guidance. If Muslims isolate themselves from society, who will carry the light to those in darkness?

Even Medina during the Prophet’s ﷺ time was not the ‘perfect Islamic bubble’ many imagine. There were Jews, hypocrites, idol worshipers, prostitution, drinking, corruption - all while the Prophet ﷺ lived there. The work of establishing Islam happened IN that messy environment, not in isolation from it.

Words Have Weight: The skeptics’ sarcastic statement ‘That would be a losing return!’ will be preserved and thrown back at them. This teaches us to be conscious of what we say. Casual mockery, sarcasm, and dismissive words about divine matters are not harmless jokes - they’re recorded, and we may have to answer for them. Guard your tongue.

The Reality of Resistance

Why Truth Faces Opposition: The passage reveals that resistance to truth is rarely purely theological. Pharaoh’s resistance was economic (he needed slave labor), political (his authority depended on religious claims), and social (his entire system would collapse). Similarly, the Quraysh’s resistance was economic (Ka’bah idol tourism) and political (tribal authority).

This teaches: when you speak truth, expect resistance not just from ‘religious’ opposition but from those whose economic interests, political power, or social status is threatened by that truth. People often use religious arguments to defend non-religious interests. Don’t be surprised or discouraged by opposition - understand its real roots.

The Test of Patience: Both Musa (AS) and Muhammad ﷺ had to persist for years with their messages before seeing victory. Musa (AS) spent years confronting Pharaoh before the Exodus. The Prophet ﷺ spent 13 years in Makkah before the Hijrah. Truth doesn’t always triumph quickly. The believer’s job is not to ‘win’ immediately but to persist in truth regardless of immediate results. Victory belongs to Allah’s timing, not ours.

Ultimate Accountability

History as Warning: Pharaoh was made an example (nakal) for later generations and earlier ones. His story is preserved as warning - this is what happens when you reject messengers and oppress people. No matter how powerful you seem, Allah’s judgment comes. For believers, this is reassurance: oppressors will be held accountable, even if we don’t see it in our lifetimes. For oppressors, it’s warning: your power is temporary; accountability is coming.

The Function of Death and Resurrection: The skeptics mock the idea of returning after becoming ‘hollow bones.’ But this reveals a spiritual truth - death and resurrection serve a purpose. If life continued indefinitely without accountability, oppressors would never face justice, and the oppressed would never see vindication. Resurrection isn’t arbitrary; it’s necessary for divine justice to be fully manifest. Every wrong will be addressed, every good deed rewarded. This gives meaning to suffering and purpose to righteousness.

Living with the End in Mind

The Sleepless Night: The imagery of ‘Sahirah’ (sleepless night) is terrifying - anxious wakefulness, anticipation, inability to rest. But this teaches us something practical: if you live with consciousness of that Day in this life (moral awareness, regular self-accounting, consciousness of Allah), you develop spiritual preparation. Those who ‘sleep’ through this life in moral negligence will be jolted awake on that Day in shock. Those who maintained consciousness in this life will not be caught off guard.

The wisdom is: live your life in this world with consciousness of the next. Don’t wait for the ‘wake-up call’ of death - wake up now, spiritually and morally. Be among those who fear Allah, who take warning, who live consciously.

Hope and Fear in Balance: The passage presents both the terror of Judgment Day and the mercy of Allah’s gentle invitation through messengers. This is the balanced Islamic psychology - we fear Allah’s justice (which motivates us to avoid wrong) and hope in His mercy (which prevents despair). Both are necessary: too much fear leads to despair, too much hope leads to complacency. The balance produces healthy spiritual motivation.

Ayah 17–26 · linked video: 03. An-Nazi’at (Ayah 17-26)

Root Word Analysis

Ayah 17

طَغَىٰ (tagha) [ط-غ-ي]

The root ط-غ-ي means to transgress bounds, to exceed limits, to rebel outrageously. The verb طَغَىٰ (tagha) in Form I conveys the meaning of extreme rebellion and oppression beyond reasonable limits. This is not mere disobedience but oppression that goes too far - outrageously oppressive behavior. The word carries multiple layers: being oppressive (الظلم), going too far in oppression (المبالغة في الظلم والجور), animosity (العداوة), and acts of aggression without justification (التعدي). When used for Pharaoh, it encompasses torture, crimes against humanity, and preemptive aggression without cause. The word suggests someone who is a dangerous, oppressive entity whose tyranny knows no bounds.

Ayah 18

تَزَكَّىٰ (tazakka) [ز-ك-ي]

The root ز-ك-ي relates to growth, purification, and betterment. The verb تَزَكَّىٰ (tazakka) in Form V (reflexive/intensive) means to purify oneself, to better oneself. The noun زَكَاة (zakah) comes from this root - the concept that something grows or betters because of goodness. Zakah in its fundamental meaning implies enhancement through removing filth - as you get away from what is filthy, it enhances you and adds blessing (baraka) in your life. This creates a cycle where Allah opens more doors for good and gives more strength to avoid evil. The process is gradual - one step after another, one stage after another. Nobody becomes perfect overnight. The first step in this purification is concern for one’s own well-being - unless you care about your own self, the journey toward guidance cannot even begin.

أَهْدِيَكَ (ahdiyaka) [ه-د-ي]

The root ه-د-ي means guidance, but its original meaning is a rock (إراخ) protruding from water - a landmark or sea marker that travelers use to know they are on the right track. From this comes guidance - clear landmarks or milestones showing the way forward. Another meaning is the bright daytime (الهُدَى) when the road is absolutely clear, in contrast to nighttime darkness when navigation is impossible. The verb أَهْدِيَكَ (ahdiyaka) in Form IV means “I will guide you” - I will show you what landmarks and milestones you should reach. Guidance (هدى) is not mystical or mysterious - it is straightforward landmarks, clear directions, observable milestones on a path that is illuminated.

رَبِّكَ (rabbika) [ر-ب-ب]

The root ر-ب-ب fundamentally means master, lord, nurturer, sustainer. The word رَبّ (rabb) can mean God, but it does not necessarily mean God exclusively - it can also mean master or supreme authority. In this context, Musa عليه السلام is inviting Pharaoh to his Master - Allah جل جلاله - the true Master as opposed to Pharaoh’s false claim of mastery over people. The distinction is crucial because Pharaoh later declares “أَنَا رَبُّكُمُ الْأَعْلَىٰ” (I am your most supreme master/lord), using the same root. The word رَبّ carries connotations of authority, lordship, and the one who deserves obedience.

تَخْشَىٰ (takhsha) [خ-ش-ي]

The root خ-ش-ي means to fear, but specifically a fear combined with reverence, awe, and consciousness. The verb تَخْشَىٰ (takhsha) means “you may fear” or “you may develop consciousness.” This is not terror but a conscious awareness that keeps one from regressing into darkness. Once you get on the road of guidance and start walking away from darkness toward light, you develop خَشْيَة (khashyah) - a fear of doing things that would make you take steps backward. This consciousness develops as you progress - it is not present at the beginning but grows as you move forward on the path of purification. It is a protective fear that keeps you advancing rather than retreating.

Ayah 20

الْكُبْرَىٰ (al-kubra) [ك-ب-ر]

The root ك-ب-ر means greatness, largeness, supremacy. The word الْكُبْرَىٰ (al-kubra) is the superlative feminine form meaning “the greatest” or “the supreme.” When Allah says فَأَرَاهُ الْآيَةَ الْكُبْرَىٰ (He showed him the greatest sign), there is scholarly debate whether this refers to the staff (عصا) transforming into a serpent or the hand (يد) changing color. The staff transforms a non-living thing into a living creature - a complete transformation of creation. The hand changes the nature of something that already exists. Some scholars argue that الْآيَةَ الْكُبْرَىٰ (the greatest sign) - using the singular - encompasses all the signs collectively as the ultimate proof, the greatest possible miracle. The use of singular does not necessarily mean only one sign but rather that taken together, these constitute the supreme evidence that cannot be surpassed.

Ayah 21

كَذَّبَ (kadhdhaba) [ك-ذ-ب]

The root ك-ذ-ب means to lie, to deny truth, to call something false. The verb كَذَّبَ (kadhdhaba) in Form II (intensive) means he persistently denied, he called it a lie, he rejected thoroughly. The Form II pattern intensifies the action - this is not a one-time denial but continuous, insistent rejection. Pharaoh called the messenger a liar, called the signs lies or trickery or magic, and denied everything that could be denied. The word is left open-ended - whether character assassination against Musa عليه السلام, dismissing the greatest signs as magic, or denying signs happening around him, he rejected all of it. He considered everything presented to him as false - “this cannot be happening.”

عَصَىٰ (asa) [ع-ص-ي]

The root ع-ص-ي means to disobey, to rebel, to refuse compliance. The verb عَصَىٰ (asa) in Form I means he disobeyed. This follows كَذَّبَ - first denial in speech, then disobedience in action. After calling the message false, he then actively disobeyed and refused to comply with what Musa عليه السلام called him to. The progression is significant: first intellectual rejection (تكذيب), then behavioral rebellion (عصيان).

Ayah 22

أَدْبَرَ (adbara) [د-ب-ر]

The root د-ب-ر means to turn away, to turn one’s back, to retreat, to scheme. The verb أَدْبَرَ (adbara) in Form IV means he turned his back, he turned away. While some classical interpretations suggest Pharaoh literally turned his back and ran when the staff became a serpent in the court, the context suggests a more figurative meaning. The word ثُمَّ (thumma - “then”) indicates something that happened much later, not immediately. After Pharaoh denied Musa عليه السلام and was presented with the great sign, he turned away - refused to hear anything Musa عليه السلام had to say again. The word دَبَّرَ also means planning and scheming. So أَدْبَرَ can mean turning away while simultaneously plotting and scheming - he is busy making efforts to undermine the message, trying to formulate a strategy to annihilate what Musa عليه السلام brought.

يَسْعَىٰ (yas’a) [س-ع-ي]

The root س-ع-ي means to strive, to make efforts, to work actively toward something, to rush about. The verb يَسْعَىٰ (yas’a) means he strives, he makes efforts, he runs around working. This is not literal running but figurative - making active efforts, scheming, working to accomplish a goal. After turning away from Musa’s message, Pharaoh is actively striving and making efforts to contain the situation, to undermine the message, to maintain his control and authority.

Ayah 23

حَشَرَ (hashara) [ح-ش-ر]

The root ح-ش-ر means to gather, to herd, to assemble forcefully. The verb حَشَرَ (hashara) in Form I means he gathered forcefully, he herded. The word حَشْر is used specifically when you gather people for war, and it is also used when you herd animals. This is a significant insight - it suggests Pharaoh considered people as animals, just herds to be controlled, not respected citizens but insects or cattle. He forcefully gathered magicians to form a dream team, and he also forced people to attend the spectacle, making it massive and public. The word reveals his contempt for people - he thought nothing of them, considered them worthless (الاستخفاف بالناس). This is how dictators think - people are insignificant, disposable.

نَادَىٰ (nada) [ن-د-ي]

The root ن-د-ي means to call out, to make a public announcement, to proclaim. The verb نَادَىٰ (nada) in Form III means he called out publicly, he made an announcement. After gathering the people and magicians, Pharaoh made a public proclamation to remind everyone of his supreme authority.

Ayah 24

الْأَعْلَىٰ (al-a’la) [ع-ل-و]

The root ع-ل-و means height, elevation, supremacy, being above. The word الْأَعْلَىٰ (al-a’la) is the superlative form meaning “the highest,” “the most supreme,” “the uppermost.” When Pharaoh declares “أَنَا رَبُّكُمُ الْأَعْلَىٰ” (I am your lord, the most supreme), he is not merely saying “I am your superior master” but “I am your MOST SUPREME master” - the superlative form. As if to say there is no room to think of any god or authority higher than himself. You cannot possibly think of anyone above him - there is no one else to speak to, no higher authority. This is the attitude of claiming godhood, but also the attitude of claiming absolute authority where no one needs to be obeyed except him. This applies not just to those who claim to be God, but to anyone who declares superiority and imposes oppressive authority, saying there is no higher authority than themselves. People can do this in families, in relationships, wherever there is a power imbalance - imposing their authority and being oppressive by declaring no one else deserves to be heard or obeyed.

Ayah 25

نَكَالَ (nakal) [ن-ك-ل]

The root ن-ك-ل means to make an example of someone through punishment, to terrorize through exemplary punishment, to restrain through fear. The word نَكَال (nakal) means an exemplary punishment, making someone a lesson for others. Originally, نِكْل referred to a cage for an animal being tortured because it was out of line - like a dog that bit its owner put in a cage and left in the sun to teach it a lesson and warn other animals. It also refers to chains and fetters. In prisons, when an inmate goes out of hand, there is a special cell with more severe torture just for that prisoner - that is نَكَال. This word is particularly powerful for Pharaoh because HE imprisoned people, made examples of people, chained and enslaved people, and humiliated and tortured those who stood against him to terrify others. Allah is saying “I will do that to YOU” - make an example of you to terrify others who rebel against Allah. The word نَكَال is used throughout the Quran for making examples of criminals to terrify others (لِتَخْوِيفِ الْغَيْرِ). Pharaoh wanted to make an example of the magicians who believed by mutilating and crucifying them publicly - Allah flipped the script and made Pharaoh the ultimate example.

الْآخِرَةِ (al-akhirah) [أ-خ-ر]

The root أ-خ-ر means last, later, end, hereafter. The word الْآخِرَة (al-akhirah) means the Hereafter, the afterlife, the final realm. When Allah says He made Pharaoh an example “نَكَالَ الْآخِرَةِ وَالْأُولَىٰ” (exemplary punishment of the Hereafter and this life), the order is significant - the afterlife is mentioned FIRST before this life. This teaches that Allah will not just punish in Hell but will also punish in this world (الدنيا/الأولى). The torture of the next life is far worse, so it is mentioned first. But people should not think “judgment day will come, then punishment will come” - no, Allah will deal with you in THIS life also. Some will pay their price in this life as well as the next.

الْأُولَىٰ (al-ula) [أ-و-ل]

The root أ-و-ل means first, earlier, former, this world. The word الْأُولَىٰ (al-ula) means the first life, this world, the earlier realm (as opposed to the Hereafter). Allah tortured Pharaoh with water in this life (drowning) and will torture him with fire in the next. The mention of afterlife first (الآخرة) followed by this life (الأولى) emphasizes that the punishment of the Hereafter is more intense and longer lasting, but there will also be consequences in this world.

Ayah 26

عِبْرَةً (ibrah) [ع-ب-ر]

The root ع-ب-ر has multiple foundational meanings. First, it means to evaluate coins by weighing them - to assess the value of currency, jewelry, and metals by their weight. Second, it means to cross over a valley or river - to traverse from one side to another. From these meanings comes the word عِبْرَة (ibrah) - a lesson, an example to learn from. But عِبْرَة is not just any lesson - it is a lesson so powerful that it moves you to tears (العَبْرة are tears, from the same root, because tears cross over from your eye down your skin). It is a lesson in which you find great value (from the weighing/evaluating meaning). It is a lesson that helps you cross from where you are to where you need to be (from the traversing meaning). So عِبْرَة implies: a lesson that should emotionally move you, a lesson that should practically move you and change something in you, and a lesson you should find truly valuable. The word عِبْرَة also implies deep contemplation (التَّفَكُّر العَمِيق) - you evaluate the example, compare it to your own experience, contemplate it, and take counsel from it.

يَخْشَىٰ (yakhsha) [خ-ش-ي]

The root خ-ش-ي means to fear with consciousness and reverence. The verb يَخْشَىٰ (yakhsha) means “who fears,” “who has consciousness,” “who chooses to be afraid.” This is the second occurrence of this root in the surah - earlier in Ayah 19 it was تَخْشَىٰ (takhsha - “you may fear”). The recurrence builds on the earlier usage. In Ayah 19, Musa عليه السلام was offering Pharaoh the possibility of developing fear and consciousness of Allah. Now in Ayah 26, Allah says this entire story serves as a lesson for anyone among the listeners who CHOOSES to be afraid, to humble themselves. The same offer made to Pharaoh is now made to the audience - to you and me. This shows the story is not just about Pharaoh but about all of us. The word يَخْشَىٰ (present tense, indicating continuous action and choice) emphasizes that fear/consciousness is a choice - “for whoever WILL CHOOSE to be afraid.” It brings the point back to personal relevance - this is about examining yourself and choosing to develop consciousness of Allah.

Grammatical & Rhetorical Nuances

Ayah 17

The phrase “إِنَّهُ طَغَىٰ” (innahu tagha - “Indeed, he rebelled”) contains إِنَّ which provides emphasis and confirmation - “certainly he is the one, in fact, that has rebelled.” The pronoun هُ (he) with emphasis from إِنَّ highlights that Pharaoh specifically is guilty.

There are two grammatical interpretations of “إِنَّهُ طَغَىٰ” functioning as explanation:

  1. As سَبَب (cause/reason): “Go to Pharaoh BECAUSE he has rebelled.” This suggests the work of Islam and the ummah is to go directly to criminals, engage them, challenge them, and give them the message of Islam. We cannot just sit and complain about them - they must be addressed directly.

  2. As إِفَادَة (clarification/emphasis): “In fact, HE is the one that rebelled” - not the other way around. This is powerful because oppressive regimes do not just commit violence; they redefine what they do as peace and what others do as aggression and crimes. When Pharaoh punishes, tortures, and kills people, he makes it sound like THEY are the criminals rebelling against the government. Allah is saying: regardless of his propaganda, HE is the criminal. This teaches us to see through the false narratives of oppressive regimes - they kill civilians while claiming to fight terrorists, outlaws, anarchists, or gangs. They dress up in nice clothes with polished press releases saying they are restoring peace and order while bombing hospitals and shooting children. “إِنَّهُ طَغَىٰ” cuts through this - it does not matter what the script officially says; look at reality. Allah determines he is guilty.

Ayah 18

The verse “هَل لَّكَ إِلَىٰ أَن تَزَكَّىٰ” (hal laka ila an tazakka) uses the interrogative particle هَل to create softness and courtesy in speech. The question format - “Would you consider even the possibility of bettering yourself?” - is gentle and inviting rather than confrontational. This softness is emphasized even more explicitly in Surah Taha (20:44) where Allah commands Musa and Harun عليهما السلام: “فَقُولَا لَهُ قَوْلًا لَّيِّنًا” (speak to him with soft, sweet speech). The word لَيِّن describes a really good date - soft, flexible, and sweet. Speak to Pharaoh as softly and sweetly as a date.

The rhetorical strategy here is crucial. When representing oppressed people and speaking to someone in political power who has committed crimes, you have built-up anger - your own and that of all your people. The moment you finally get to meet this person, there is tremendous temptation to scream, yell, or throw something. But what happens? You are immediately removed from the audience. You accomplish nothing except blowing hot air. The manner of speech must remain soft, collected, and calm so you do not come across as an erratic, angry maniac. By keeping your composure, eventually the oppressor will lose his cool and start looking like a fool - you win by remaining calm. This is especially significant for Musa عليه السلام who has a serious temper and can take matters into his own hands (as seen when he killed a man with one punch). He himself acknowledged this issue: “وَيَضِيقُ صَدْرِي” (my chest becomes tight - when Pharaoh says obnoxious things like “I am God,” I will get upset, my chest will tighten, and my tongue will stop working, meaning my hands will do the talking instead). So Allah gave him Harun عليه السلام to help him remain calm and contained.

The tactical lesson: those who represent Islam in media or who will speak with power must be people who can control themselves, remain calm, stay on point, and not be swayed by emotion. Master manipulators like Pharaoh will try to get under your skin, make you lose composure so you look like the lunatic while they appear reasonable.

The progression in the verse is also strategic: “هَل لَّكَ إِلَىٰ أَن تَزَكَّىٰ * وَأَهْدِيَكَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ فَتَخْشَىٰ” - first, concern for your own purification (self-interest), then guidance to your Master (knowledge and direction), then fear/consciousness (spiritual growth). Each stage builds on the previous. Unless you care about your own well-being, the journey cannot begin. Once on that road learning what will purify you, you develop fear - not just fear of Allah but fear of regressing, fear of taking steps backward after moving forward.

Ayah 20

The use of the singular “الْآيَةَ الْكُبْرَىٰ” (the greatest sign) despite there being multiple signs (staff, hand, and others) is rhetorically significant. It can suggest that all the signs collectively constitute the ultimate proof - there does not need to be a plural form because together they are THE supreme miracle, the greatest possible evidence. Alternatively, the singular can indicate one supreme sign above all others, though scholars debated which one (the transformation of the staff into a living serpent from non-living matter, or the hand changing color/nature).

Ayah 21

The sequence “فَكَذَّبَ وَعَصَىٰ” (he denied and he disobeyed) shows a progression from intellectual rejection to behavioral rebellion. The conjunction وَ (and) links denial in speech with disobedience in action. The open-ended nature of “كَذَّبَ” (he denied) - without specifying exactly what he denied - is rhetorically powerful because it encompasses everything: character assassination of Musa عليه السلام, dismissing miracles as magic/trickery, denying signs happening around him. Whatever could be denied, he denied.

Ayah 22

The word “ثُمَّ” (then) at the beginning of “ثُمَّ أَدْبَرَ يَسْعَىٰ” indicates something that happened much later (not immediately), which is significant for interpretation. This suggests أَدْبَرَ (turning away) is not literal running from the snake in the court (which would be immediate) but figurative turning away from the message afterward, followed by scheming and plotting (يَسْعَىٰ - making efforts).

The pairing of أَدْبَرَ (turning away) with يَسْعَىٰ (striving/making efforts) creates a picture of someone who refuses to engage with the message anymore but is actively working against it - turning his back while simultaneously plotting.

Ayah 23

The verb “فَحَشَرَ” (he gathered) is left without an explicit object - it does not immediately state WHAT he gathered. This creates suspense and emphasizes the action itself. The following verb “فَنَادَىٰ” (then he called out) continues the sequence of actions building toward the climactic declaration in the next verse.

Ayah 24

The declaration “أَنَا رَبُّكُمُ الْأَعْلَىٰ” (I am your lord, the most supreme) is structured for maximum rhetorical impact. The pronoun أَنَا (I) comes first for emphasis - I, myself, personally. The possessive كُمْ (your) makes it direct and personal to the audience. The superlative الْأَعْلَىٰ (the highest/most supreme) is placed at the end as the climax - not just master, not just superior, but THE MOST SUPREME with no possibility of anyone higher.

Ayah 25

The reversed order “نَكَالَ الْآخِرَةِ وَالْأُولَىٰ” (exemplary punishment of the Hereafter and the first life) is rhetorically significant - mentioning afterlife before this life. Normally chronological order would be الأولى ثم الآخرة (first this life, then the Hereafter). But Allah mentions الآخرة first because its punishment is far more intense and longer lasting - it deserves priority and emphasis. However, by including الأولى (this life), Allah ensures people do not think punishment is only in the Hereafter - there are consequences in this world too.

The word نَكَال is especially powerful for Pharaoh specifically because he imprisoned, chained, enslaved, humiliated, and made examples of people to terrorize others. Allah is using the same terminology to say “I will do to you what you did to others” - poetic justice, flipping the script.

Ayah 26

The phrase “إِنَّ فِي ذَٰلِكَ لَعِبْرَةً” (indeed in that is a lesson) uses إِنَّ for emphasis and لَ for additional emphasis (لام التوكيد) - “indeed… certainly there is a lesson.” The demonstrative pronoun ذَٰلِكَ (that) refers back to the entire account of Pharaoh - all of the preceding story.

“لِّمَن يَخْشَىٰ” (for whoever fears) uses the relative pronoun لِمَن (for whoever) making it universal and ongoing. The present tense verb يَخْشَىٰ indicates continuous action and choice - not “for whoever feared once” but “for whoever chooses to fear, for whoever will fear.”

The recurrence of the root خ-ش-ي (خشية) creates a bookend effect with Ayah 19 (فَتَخْشَىٰ). In Ayah 19, Musa عليه السلام is speaking to Pharaoh about developing fear/consciousness. In Ayah 26, Allah is speaking to the audience about the same fear/consciousness. This parallel structure brings the point home: the offer made to Pharaoh is now made to you. This story is about you, not just about him.

Historical & Biblical Context

Ayah 17

Faraʿawn (Pharaoh) was the oppressive ruler of Egypt during the time of Musa عليه السلام. The historical context shows him as someone who enslaved the Children of Israel (Bani Israel), killed their male children, and claimed divinity. The command to Musa عليه السلام to go to Pharaoh despite his tyranny shows that prophets were sent directly to confront the worst oppressors of their time. This was not theoretical preaching but direct engagement with political power.

The description of Pharaoh’s rebellion (طَغَىٰ) fits the historical accounts where he not only oppressed the Israelites but also made himself an object of worship, demanding absolute submission from his subjects. The historical Pharaoh created a system where dissent was crushed, people were imprisoned, and public executions served as warnings.

Ayah 18-19

The instruction to speak softly to Pharaoh (mentioned in Surah Taha 20:44 - “فَقُولَا لَهُ قَوْلًا لَّيِّنًا”) is particularly striking given the historical context. Musa عليه السلام had personal reasons for anger - he grew up in Pharaoh’s household and witnessed the oppression firsthand. His own mother and family suffered under this regime. The woman who raised him (Asiya رضي الله عنها) was herself being tortured by Pharaoh. Despite all this, Allah commanded soft speech.

Historically, this shows the prophetic methodology was not based on revenge or emotional reaction but on strategic da’wah. The goal was to deliver the message effectively, not to express anger or humiliate the oppressor (even though he deserved it).

Musa’s admission about his temper (“وَيَضِيقُ صَدْرِي” - my chest becomes tight, and I fear my tongue will not work) references his past when he killed an Egyptian with a single punch (Surah Al-Qasas 28:15). This historical incident shows Musa عليه السلام was aware of his weakness and needed support from his brother Harun عليه السلام to maintain composure.

Ayah 20

The “greatest sign” (الْآيَةَ الْكُبْرَىٰ) refers to the miracles shown to Pharaoh in his court. The staff of Musa عليه السلام transformed into a serpent (described in various surahs as ثُعْبَان - a python, حَيَّة - a snake with fangs), and his hand changed color to brilliant white. These events occurred in the royal court of Egypt before Pharaoh, his ministers, and magicians.

Historically, Egyptian culture was deeply involved in magic and sorcery. The magicians were considered the elite learned class. For them to be challenged in their own domain, in the court, was strategically significant. The transformation of the staff was not just a miracle but a direct challenge to Egyptian religious and magical authority.

Ayah 21

Pharaoh’s response to the signs - calling them lies and magic - is recorded in other parts of the Quran. In Surah Yunus (10:76-77), he calls it “سِحْرٌ” (magic). Despite witnessing undeniable miracles, he resorted to character assassination of Musa عليه السلام, trying to frame him as a threat to homeland security (mentioned in Surah Ghafir 40:26 - “إِنِّي أَخَافُ أَن يُبَدِّلَ دِينَكُمْ” - I fear he will change your religion, and “أَن يُظْهِرَ فِي الْأَرْضِ الْفَسَادَ” - that he will cause corruption in the land).

This historical pattern of oppressive regimes labeling truth-tellers as threats, terrorists, or dangers to national security has repeated throughout history and continues today.

Ayah 22

After being humiliated in his own court, Pharaoh needed to contain the situation. Historically, he could not simply imprison Musa عليه السلام immediately because: (1) it would make him appear weak and afraid, (2) Musa عليه السلام would preach to other prisoners who were already rebels against Pharaoh, creating a secret army, (3) generals and commanders might sympathize with Musa عليه السلام and visit him in prison.

Pharaoh’s strategy (يَسْعَىٰ - his scheming and efforts) was to gather the best magicians from across Egypt and arrange a massive public spectacle to defeat Musa عليه السلام publicly, thereby restoring his authority and discrediting the message. This is detailed in Surah Taha (20:59-64) and Surah Ash-Shu’ara (26:36-42) where the famous confrontation takes place.

Ayah 23

Historically, Pharaoh gathered (حَشَرَ) both the magicians and the general population for what became a massive public event - the Day of Festival (يَوْمُ الزِّينَةِ mentioned in Surah Taha 20:59). The word حَشَرَ implies forced gathering, like herding. People did not come voluntarily but were compelled to attend this spectacle. This was Pharaoh’s attempt to stage-manage a public relations victory.

The gathering of magicians from various cities created a “dream team” of sorcerers to collectively face Musa عليه السلام. In other surahs, we learn they were offered rewards and honors if they succeeded.

Ayah 24

Pharaoh’s declaration “أَنَا رَبُّكُمُ الْأَعْلَىٰ” (I am your most supreme lord) represents the culmination of his claims to divinity, which are mentioned in multiple places in the Quran. In Surah Al-Qasas (28:38), he says “مَا عَلِمْتُ لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَٰهٍ غَيْرِي” (I know of no god for you other than me). This was not just theological heresy but a political declaration of absolute authority - no one had the right to question or disobey him.

Historically, this reflects the Egyptian Pharaonic tradition where rulers were considered divine or semi-divine intermediaries between the gods and people. The claim to absolute authority was both religious and political.

Ayah 25-26

The punishment of Pharaoh in this life refers to his drowning in the sea when pursuing Bani Israel. This event is detailed extensively in Surah Yunus (10:90-92) where his body was preserved as a sign for future generations. The Quran explicitly states: “فَالْيَوْمَ نُنَجِّيكَ بِبَدَنِكَ لِتَكُونَ لِمَنْ خَلْفَكَ آيَةً” (So today We will save your body so that you may be a sign for those who come after you).

The drowning in water (الْأُولَىٰ - this life) contrasts with burning in Fire (الْآخِرَةِ - the Hereafter). Historically, the escape of Bani Israel through the parted Red Sea and the drowning of Pharaoh’s army is one of the most significant miracles recorded in Abrahamic scripture. The Quran mentions it repeatedly as a sign (آية) and lesson (عِبْرَة) for all generations.

The mention of Pharaoh’s story as عِبْرَة (a profound lesson) acknowledges that this is not just ancient history but a timeless example. The Quran presents this account to the Arabs of Makkah who were themselves facing oppression from the Quraysh leadership, offering both warning to oppressors and hope to the oppressed.

Coherence

Ayahs 17-26 within the Surah

These verses constitute the narrative section of Surah An-Nazi’at. The surah opened with powerful oaths about angels extracting souls (Ayahs 1-5), then scenes from the Day of Resurrection (Ayahs 6-14), then the famous statement by the disbelievers “أَإِنَّا لَمَرْدُودُونَ فِي الْحَافِرَةِ” (Will we really be restored to our former state?) - expressing disbelief in resurrection (Ayah 10-11).

After establishing the reality of resurrection and judgment, Allah now presents the historical example of Pharaoh (Ayahs 17-26) as proof that: (1) resurrection and accountability are real, (2) those who deny and rebel will face consequences both in this life and the next, (3) even the mightiest tyrant cannot escape Allah’s justice.

The Pharaoh narrative serves as a concrete example of the abstract theological concepts introduced earlier. Where the opening spoke of souls being extracted violently or gently, here we see the ultimate example of a soul that will be extracted violently - Pharaoh’s. Where earlier verses spoke of hearts trembling on Judgment Day, here we see what leads to that fate: pride, denial, oppression.

The story of Musa عليه السلام confronting Pharaoh demonstrates that the call to believe in Allah, resurrection, and accountability is not new - it has been the message of all prophets. The denial is also not new - previous generations, even when shown the greatest miracles, refused to believe.

Connection to Surrounding Passages

After the Pharaoh account (Ayahs 17-26), the surah will shift to reflecting on the creation of the heavens and earth (Ayahs 27-33) as further evidence of Allah’s power to resurrect. The progression is logical: first the promise of resurrection, then the historical example of one who denied and was destroyed, then the cosmic evidence of Allah’s creative power.

The phrase “إِنَّ فِي ذَٰلِكَ لَعِبْرَةً لِّمَن يَخْشَىٰ” (Indeed in that is a lesson for whoever fears) in Ayah 26 serves as a transition. It closes the Pharaoh narrative while opening the door to broader reflection. The word “يَخْشَىٰ” (fears/has consciousness) connects back to “فَتَخْشَىٰ” in Ayah 19, creating thematic unity.

Thematic Links to Other Quranic Passages

The Pharaoh narrative appears in multiple surahs with varying emphases: - Surah Taha (20:9-98): Most detailed account including dialogue, the confrontation with magicians, the parting of the sea - Surah Ash-Shu’ara (26:10-68): Focuses on the dialogue and public spectacle - Surah Al-Qasas (28:3-43): Focuses on Musa’s early life and his return to Egypt - Surah Yunus (10:75-92): Focuses on Pharaoh’s drowning and the preservation of his body - Surah Ghafir (40:23-46): Includes the story of the believing man from Pharaoh’s family

Each retelling emphasizes different aspects. In Surah An-Nazi’at, the emphasis is on: (1) the ultimate rebellion (طَغَىٰ), (2) the soft approach despite his crimes, (3) his denial despite clear evidence, (4) his ultimate punishment as a lesson (عِبْرَة).

The instruction to speak softly (implicitly referenced here, explicitly in Surah Taha) is emphasized in this surah because the broader theme is about divine mercy even to the worst of criminals - they are given a chance, approached with respect, offered guidance. This magnifies their guilt when they reject.

Word Recurrences Creating Coherence

The root خ-ش-ي appears twice (Ayahs 19 and 26), creating a frame around the narrative. It begins with offering Pharaoh the path to fear/consciousness of Allah (فَتَخْشَىٰ) and ends with the lesson being for anyone who chooses such fear (لِّمَن يَخْشَىٰ). This repetition emphasizes that consciousness of Allah is the key lesson.

The root ر-ب-ب appears in both Musa’s invitation (“رَبِّكَ” - your Lord, Ayah 19) and Pharaoh’s false claim (“رَبُّكُمُ” - your lord, Ayah 24). This contrast highlights the central theological conflict: who is the true Master/Lord? Musa عليه السلام calls to the true Rabb, Pharaoh falsely claims to be rabb.

The root ك-ب-ر (greatness) appears in “الْآيَةَ الْكُبْرَىٰ” (the greatest sign, Ayah 20) and conceptually contrasts with “الْأَعْلَىٰ” (the highest/most supreme, Ayah 24). The greatest sign pointed to Allah’s supremacy, but Pharaoh claimed to be the most supreme - direct contradiction.

Structural Balance

The passage has a clear structure: - Introduction/Command: Go to Pharaoh for he has rebelled (Ayah 17) - The Offer: Invitation to purification, guidance, and fear of Allah (Ayahs 18-19) - The Evidence: Greatest sign shown (Ayah 20) - The Response: Denial and disobedience (Ayah 21) - The Consequence: Scheming, gathering, false proclamation (Ayahs 22-24) - The Punishment: Exemplary destruction in both lives (Ayah 25) - The Lesson: Application to the audience (Ayah 26)

This structure moves from divine command → prophetic invitation → miraculous proof → human rejection → human plotting → divine justice → universal lesson. It is a complete narrative arc in just 10 verses.

Psychological, Political & Social

Ayah 17

Political Dynamics of Engaging Power

The command “اذْهَبْ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ” (Go to Pharaoh) establishes a crucial principle: Islam does not permit remaining silent about oppression or merely complaining from the sidelines. Those who represent the truth must directly engage with those in power, even the most dangerous criminals. This is not optional activism but a fundamental duty.

Psychologically, this confronts our natural tendency to avoid conflict and remain in comfortable echo chambers. The Muslim community cannot just talk about oppressors among themselves - they must be willing to address oppressors directly, challenge them publicly, and present the message of truth to power.

The phrase “إِنَّهُ طَغَىٰ” (indeed he has rebelled) teaches a profound lesson about the nature of oppressive power: it redefines reality. Oppressive regimes do not simply commit violence; they control the narrative. They call their violence “peace-keeping,” their aggression “defense,” their victims “terrorists” or “criminals” or “threats to national security.” Pharaoh, when confronted by Musa عليه السلام, claimed that Musa was the danger to Egypt, that Musa wanted to expel people from their homeland. This inversion of reality is a universal tool of tyranny.

Psychologically, this teaches Muslims to see through propaganda. Do not accept the official story at face value. When a government bombs hospitals and calls it “fighting terrorism,” when it imprisons dissenters and calls it “maintaining order,” remember: “إِنَّهُ طَغَىٰ” - HE is the criminal, regardless of what he says.

Ayahs 18-19

Psychology of Change and Rehabilitation

The invitation “هَل لَّكَ إِلَىٰ أَن تَزَكَّىٰ” (Would you like to purify yourself?) reveals Allah’s psychological approach to transformation. Even to Pharaoh - the worst oppressor - the offer begins with his own self-interest: “This is for YOUR benefit. This will purify YOU.”

Psychologically, people are motivated by self-interest. Before you can call someone to serve God or help others, you must first awaken their concern for their own well-being. Pharaoh had lost all concern for himself - he had no fear of consequence even for his own soul. Unless that basic concern is rekindled, transformation is impossible.

The progression from تَزَكَّىٰ (self-purification) to أَهْدِيَكَ (I will guide you) to تَخْشَىٰ (you will fear) maps the psychological journey of spiritual growth: 1. First, care about your own state (self-awareness) 2. Then seek knowledge and direction (learning) 3. Finally develop consciousness that prevents regression (ongoing commitment)

This is realistic psychology - nobody becomes perfect overnight. Change happens in stages. You do not need to be an angel tomorrow, just take one step. The focus on gradual progress (“the road to guidance”) prevents the paralyzing perfectionism that stops people from even starting.

The Strategy of Soft Speech

The command to speak softly (قَوْلًا لَّيِّنًا) despite Pharaoh’s crimes is both tactical and psychological.

Tactically: Anger and aggression lead to immediate removal from the conversation. You accomplish nothing by venting rage except personal catharsis. But if you maintain composure, you retain access. Eventually, the oppressor will lose his composure (as Pharaoh did), and then YOU look reasonable while HE looks unhinged.

Psychologically: When you remain calm in the face of someone else’s crimes and arrogance, it (1) gives them space to actually hear what you are saying rather than triggering defensiveness, (2) demonstrates moral superiority without claiming it verbally, (3) protects you from being manipulated into an emotional reaction that discredits you.

This requires enormous self-control, especially for someone like Musa عليه السلام who had a temper and had personally witnessed these injustices. The lesson: controlling yourself is a prerequisite for effectively challenging power.

Socially: The way you speak to power affects not just the conversation with the oppressor but the perception of observers. Guards, servants, officials watching the exchange will be influenced. If you appear calm and reasonable while the tyrant appears defensive and threatened, you win the observers even if you do not convince the tyrant.

Ayahs 20-21

Psychology of Denial

Despite being shown “الْآيَةَ الْكُبْرَىٰ” (the greatest sign), Pharaoh “كَذَّبَ وَعَصَىٰ” (denied and disobeyed). This reveals a profound psychological truth: evidence alone does not convince everyone. Some people, no matter how clear the truth, will deny it because acknowledging it threatens their identity, power, or worldview.

The psychology of denial operates through: - Cognitive dissonance: Accepting the truth would contradict everything Pharaoh built his life and rule upon - Sunk cost fallacy: Pharaoh had invested too much in his claims to divinity to back down - Motivated reasoning: When the conclusion (“I am not god”) is unbearable, the mind finds ways to reject evidence

The lesson: Do not be shocked when clear evidence does not convince everyone. Some will see water part before their eyes and still deny. This is not about lack of evidence but about the psychology of those who have too much invested in falsehood.

Politically: This also explains why some regimes continue policies that are clearly failing - admitting error would undermine their legitimacy, so they double down on denial.

Ayahs 22-24

Sociology of Tyranny

The word “حَشَرَ” (he gathered/herded) contains a devastating social insight: tyrants view people as animals to be herded, not citizens to be served. This is described as “الِاسْتِخْفَاف بِالنَّاس” (thinking lightly of people, considering them worthless).

Sociology of oppression: - Dehumanization: The oppressor stops seeing the oppressed as fully human, which psychologically permits atrocities - Expendability: When you view people as insects or herds, their suffering does not register as significant - Control through fear: Making examples of dissenters (as Pharaoh did with the magicians - threatening to mutilate and crucify them publicly) terrorizes others into submission

This mindset is not limited to ancient dictators. The transcript powerfully notes: You do not need a pyramid to be a Pharaoh. The wealthy person who treats servants like trash, speaking to a 70-year-old man like garbage while being served by him. Parents who treat weak or elderly family members as burdens. In-laws who treat daughters-in-law as inferior. Anyone in a position of power who treats those under them with contempt and cruelty embodies Pharaonic qualities.

The social lesson: Watch how people treat those who have no power over them - servants, subordinates, the vulnerable. This reveals their true character. Someone may be charming to peers and superiors but cruel to those below them - that is a Pharaonic trait.

Political Spectacle

Pharaoh’s gathering of magicians and forcing people to attend the public confrontation (يَوْمُ الزِّينَةِ - the Day of Festival) is classic political theater: - Stage-manage public events to create the appearance of popular support - Use spectacle to distract from substance - Turn truth-seeking into entertainment - Control the narrative by controlling the venue and audience

Modern parallels: Propaganda rallies, televised trials, public executions - all designed to demonstrate power and intimidate opposition. Authoritarian regimes still use mass gatherings to create the illusion of unanimous support.

Pharaoh’s declaration “أَنَا رَبُّكُمُ الْأَعْلَىٰ” (I am your most supreme lord) represents the ultimate political claim: absolute authority with no accountability to any higher power. This is not just theological heresy but political totalitarianism - “No one has the right to question me. There is no authority above me. Submit or face consequences.”

Psychologically, such claims require constant reinforcement because deep down, the tyrant knows they are false. Hence the need for spectacles, propaganda, and crushing dissent - not because the regime is strong, but because it is insecure.

Ayah 25

Justice and Accountability

The phrase “فَأَخَذَهُ اللَّهُ نَكَالَ الْآخِرَةِ وَالْأُولَىٰ” (So Allah seized him with exemplary punishment of the Hereafter and this life) establishes a crucial principle: accountability is not deferred only to the afterlife. Oppressors may face consequences in this world too.

Psychologically, this provides hope to the oppressed: Justice may come in your lifetime, not only after death. Socially, it warns the oppressors: You may get away with it for a while, but your time will come, and when it does, the fall will be spectacular.

The poetic justice (نَكَال) of Pharaoh being made an example is striking - he made examples of others (threatening to crucify the magicians publicly), and Allah made an example of him. He imprisoned others, and Allah imprisoned him in a watery grave. He claimed to be supreme, and Allah utterly humiliated him.

Socially, this has a deterrent effect - when tyrants eventually fall, it sends a message to would-be tyrants. The preservation of Pharaoh’s body (mentioned in Surah Yunus 10:92) serves as a permanent visual reminder across generations.

Ayah 26

Psychology of Learning from Examples

The phrase “إِنَّ فِي ذَٰلِكَ لَعِبْرَةً لِّمَن يَخْشَىٰ” (Indeed in that is a lesson for whoever fears/has consciousness) identifies the key psychological factor: willingness to learn. The lesson is available to everyone, but only those who “يَخْشَىٰ” (choose to fear/have consciousness) will actually learn from it.

Psychology of learning: - Openness: You must be willing to see yourself in the story - Humility: You must acknowledge you could have similar qualities - Self-awareness: You must evaluate yourself honestly against the example

The transcript beautifully explains the challenge: We tend to distance ourselves from Quranic examples - “I am not THAT good” (referring to prophets) or “I am not THAT bad” (referring to villains like Pharaoh). This prevents us from learning.

The solution: These are not all-or-nothing archetypes. They are patterns. You may not be entirely like Pharaoh, but do you have some Pharaonic traits? Do you dismiss sincere advice like he dismissed Musa عليه السلام? Do you treat people under you with contempt like he treated his subjects? Do you refuse to admit error when confronted with truth?

Similarly with prophets - you are not as great as them, but can you learn from their struggles? Ya’qub عليه السلام had problems with his children - so your parenting struggles do not make you a failure. Musa عليه السلام had a temper - so your anger issues do not disqualify you from serving Allah. The examples are there to give hope and relatability, not to create impossible standards.

Socially, this teaches: When studying history or Quran, do not just read as an observer. Read as a participant - “What does this teach ME? How does this apply to MY life? What should I change about MYSELF based on this?”

The emphasis on “لِّمَن يَخْشَىٰ” (for whoever fears) suggests that those without consciousness will read the same story and learn nothing. They will say “That is about Pharaoh. I am not Pharaoh. Next page.” But those with consciousness will tremble and reflect: “Could I be heading in that direction? What in me resembles this? What must I change?”

This is the difference between reading Quran for information versus transformation - information comes automatically; transformation requires خَشْيَة (consciousness, fear, reverence).

Quranic Wisdom

Ayahs 17-26: Universal Lessons

Guidance is Simple, Not Mystical

One of the most liberating insights from this passage is the simplicity of guidance. We have created complicated manuals of purification with hundreds of steps, making Islam seem impossibly difficult. But when the Prophet ﷺ was asked for guidance, he gave simple, straightforward advice: “Say salaam and feed people.” “Do not get angry.” “Say La ilaha illa Allah and be firm upon it.” He did not give exhaustive lists.

The wisdom: Islam does not burden you with 400 rules for purifying your tongue and 500 steps for cleansing your heart. The fundamentals are simple - stay away from major evils, develop good character, treat people well, worship Allah. These are not many; they are clear and accessible.

The progression “هَل لَّكَ إِلَىٰ أَن تَزَكَّىٰ * وَأَهْدِيَكَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ فَتَخْشَىٰ” (Would you purify yourself? I will guide you to your Master so you may fear) teaches that guidance has clear milestones: 1. Care about your own well-being 2. Learn the path 3. Develop consciousness

These are not mystical stages requiring decades of seclusion. They are accessible to everyone. You know what is right and wrong. Everyone knows. The prophets from history until now pointed out the same evils. Getting away from those evils and moving toward good is the foundation. Everything else - the various adhkar, sunnah practices, enhanced worship - is icing on the cake. The cake itself is fundamental character and behavior.

The Danger of Impossible Standards

We often hear extreme examples - Abu Bakr رضي الله عنه gave everything, Umar رضي الله عنه gave half. Then we feel: “I can never reach that level, so why try?” Or we hear about a Sahabi who prayed tahajjud every night and fasted every day, and we think: “That is way beyond me.”

The wisdom: These extreme examples are given for a reason, but not to make you feel hopeless. They are there to show that even the best people had struggles. Ya’qub عليه السلام had trouble with his children - so if you struggle with your kids, you are not a failure. Musa عليه السلام had a temper - so if you get angry, you are not disqualified from righteousness. The Prophet ﷺ had marital tensions - so if you have arguments with your spouse, that does not make you a bad person.

The purpose of extreme examples is to find hope, not distance. If they can have those problems, you can too. If they overcame challenges, you can too. Do not say “They were better, so their standards do not apply to me.” Instead say “They were human like me, so I can learn from them.”

Nobody is Too Far Gone

The fact that Musa عليه السلام was sent to Pharaoh - THE Pharaoh, who claimed to be god, who killed babies, who enslaved a nation - with an invitation to purify himself teaches that no one is beyond the reach of Allah’s mercy. The door of repentance and guidance is open even to the worst oppressor.

This is profound hope for two groups: 1. Those who feel they have sinned too much: If Pharaoh was offered guidance, so are you. Do not think “I have done too many bad things; Allah will not accept me.” Even Pharaoh was given a chance.

  1. Those dealing with difficult people: Do not give up on anyone. Speak to them with soft speech. Give them the message. You do not know - maybe their heart will turn.

Of course, the flip side is also true: Just because the offer is made does not mean it will be accepted. Pharaoh rejected despite seeing the greatest miracles. Some people will remain in denial no matter what. But that is not your responsibility. Your responsibility is to deliver the message with wisdom and good character.

Consequences are Real, in This Life and the Next

The phrase “نَكَالَ الْآخِرَةِ وَالْأُولَىٰ” (exemplary punishment of the Hereafter and this life) destroys the false comfort of thinking “I will enjoy this life and deal with consequences later.” No - consequences can come in this life too. Allah will not wait until Judgment Day to deal with oppressors. History shows tyrants falling, empires collapsing, the arrogant being humiliated in their own lifetimes.

For the oppressed, this is hope: Justice may come sooner than you think. For the oppressor, this is a warning: Your time is limited. For everyone, this is a reminder: Do not think you can escape accountability by avoiding it for now. It will catch up with you, in this life or the next, or both.

The Power of Reflection (Ibrah)

The word “عِبْرَة” (ibrah - lesson/example) teaches that the Quran is not just information to be read but examples to be contemplated. When you read about Pharaoh, you should: 1. Find value in the lesson (like evaluating precious metal) 2. Be emotionally moved (like tears crossing down your face) 3. Cross over from where you are to where you need to be (transformation)

This requires looking at yourself honestly and asking: Do I have any qualities that resemble this? When truth is brought to me, do I dismiss it like Pharaoh dismissed Musa عليه السلام? When I am corrected, do I become defensive like he became defensive? When I have power over others (children, employees, servants, family members), do I treat them with contempt like he treated his subjects?

The Quran gives you these examples not to judge others but to examine yourself. This is “عِبْرَة” - taking personal counsel from the example.

The Test of Character is How You Treat the Powerless

The insight that “حَشَرَ” (herding people like animals) reveals how Pharaoh viewed his subjects teaches a timeless truth: You can judge a person’s character by how they treat those who have no power over them.

Do you treat waiters, cleaners, servants, subordinates with respect? Or do you treat them like they are beneath you? Do you speak kindly to your elderly parents even when they become a burden? Or do you tell them to stop bothering you? Do you treat your children with patience and dignity? Or do you yell at them like they are nuisances?

Phraonic qualities are not limited to political leaders. They can exist in a two-bedroom apartment. Anyone who treats people under them as if they are worthless, who speaks down to those who cannot respond, who is kind to superiors but cruel to subordinates - that person has Pharaonic traits.

The wisdom: Watch yourself in moments when you have power. That is when your true character shows.

Redefining Success and Failure

We worry about superficial standards: “My child has not memorized Quran yet. My child does not wake up for Fajr without me waking them. They do not come to the masjid before the adhan.” These parents are complaining to Ustadh Nouman about children who actually pray, actually go to the masjid, actually stay away from drugs and bad company.

The wisdom: Our standards have become artificially high in trivial matters while being artificially low in fundamental matters. A young man who prays five times a day and stays away from major sins - that is HUGE. That is success. Do not minimize it because he also plays video games or basketball. Let him be a human being.

Guidance is not about perfecting rituals while ignoring character. Guidance is about staying away from major evils, treating people well, and worshipping Allah - the fundamentals. Everything else builds on that foundation, but you cannot skip the foundation and focus on decorations.

The Reality of Oppressive Propaganda

The insight that “إِنَّهُ طَغَىٰ” can mean “In fact, HE is the one who rebelled” (emphasis on he, not others) teaches us to see through narrative manipulation. Oppressive regimes do not just commit violence; they control the story. They call themselves peacekeepers while dropping bombs. They call dissenters terrorists while they are the ones terrorizing.

The wisdom: Do not accept official narratives at face value. Look at actions, not words. When governments say they are fighting for freedom while crushing freedoms, when they say they are defending security while creating insecurity, remember Pharaoh. He said Musa عليه السلام was a danger to homeland security while he himself was the greatest danger.

Develop critical thinking. Question propaganda. Do not let the powerful control your perception of reality just because they control media and messaging.

The Strategy of Composure

The command to speak softly despite justified anger teaches a practical life skill: composure is power. When you maintain your calm in the face of provocation, you win. When you lose your cool, you lose the conversation.

This applies everywhere: - Dealing with difficult bosses: Stay calm and professional even when they are unreasonable - Facing family conflicts: Control your emotions even when others are yelling - Engaging in debates: Remain rational even when opponents become emotional - Confronting injustice: Speak firmly but with composure, do not let anger make you look unhinged

The wisdom: Anger might feel powerful in the moment, but composure is actually more powerful. It keeps you in the conversation, maintains your dignity, and often causes your opponent to lose their composure - which makes them look bad, not you.

Growth is Gradual

The concept that تَزَكَّىٰ (purification) is a process, with enhancement through removing filth that then brings more baraka, which then gives you more strength to remove more filth - this is the cycle of growth.

The wisdom: Do not expect overnight transformation. Do not think “I will become perfect by next week.” Take one step, then another. As you get away from bad habits, Allah gives you strength for the next step. As you do one good deed, He opens doors for more. Trust the process.

Nobody starts praying tahajjud on day one. Maybe day one you just start praying Fajr. Then you add consistency. Then you add other sunnah prayers. Then you improve your khushu’. Growth is gradual. Respect the process and do not burden yourself with trying to do everything at once.

Ayah 27–46 · linked video: 04. An- Nazi’at (Ayah 27 - 46)

Root Word Analysis

Ayah 27

أَأَنتُمْ (a’antum) [أ-ن-ت]

The root أ-ن-ت forms the pronoun “you” (plural). The doubling of the hamza (أَأَ) creates an emphatic interrogative particle that challenges and confronts. This interrogative form isn’t seeking information but rather making a powerful rhetorical challenge: “Are YOU really…?” The emphasis highlights the absurdity of the comparison being made.

أَشَدُّ (ashaddu) [ش-د-د]

The root ش-د-د means intensity, strength, and power. The form أَشَدُّ is the comparative/superlative (ism tafdhil), meaning “more intense” or “more powerful.” This word can refer to both the intensity of creation (more difficult to create) or the power of the creator (more powerful at creating). This dual meaning allows the ayah to work on multiple levels - questioning both Allah’s creative power and humanity’s presumption.

خَلْقًا (khalqan) [خ-ل-ق]

The root خ-ل-ق means to create, to bring into existence from nothing. The word خَلْق is the verbal noun (masdar), meaning “creation” or “the act of creating.” This encompasses both the process and the result of creation. Allah uses this root to emphasize His role as the ultimate Creator.

السَّمَاءُ (as-sama’u) [س-م-و]

The root س-م-و means height, elevation, and that which is above. السَّمَاء refers to the sky, the heavens, the canopy above. It represents everything that towers above the earth - the vast expanse that humans look up to. The definite article “al” makes it THE sky, the ultimate example of grand construction.

بَنَاهَا (banaha) [ب-ن-ي]

The root ب-ن-ي means to build, to construct, to establish with purpose and design. Unlike خَلَقَ (to create), بَنَى emphasizes the architectural aspect - deliberate construction with planning and structure. The verb form (past tense with attached pronoun) means “He built it.” This word choice is crucial because it directly challenges human construction achievements by comparing them to Allah’s construction of the sky.

Ayah 28

رَفَعَ (rafa’a) [ر-ف-ع]

The root ر-ف-ع means to elevate, to raise up, to lift high. This verb emphasizes the vertical dimension - Allah elevated the sky to tremendous heights. This relates to the architectural marvel of high ceilings that ancient civilizations prided themselves on, particularly the Egyptians with their impossibly tall columns.

سَمْكَهَا (samkaha) [س-م-ك]

The root س-م-ك means thickness, ceiling, roof. السَّمْك specifically refers to the roof or ceiling of something. The attached pronoun “ha” refers back to the sky. This word emphasizes the sky as a ceiling or canopy - something that stands above and covers. Ancient palaces built high ceilings and painted them with stars to mimic the sky.

فَسَوَّاهَا (fasawwaha) [س-و-ي]

The root س-و-ي means to make equal, to even out, to balance perfectly. The form II verb سَوَّى (sawwa) means to make something completely even and balanced. The fa- prefix indicates sequence (“and then”). Allah made the sky’s ceiling perfectly balanced and stable - it doesn’t shift, crack, or cave in despite its vast expanse. This perfection is contrasted with human construction which always has flaws and requires support.

Ayah 29

أَغْطَشَ (aghtasha) [غ-ط-ش]

The root غ-ط-ش means to cause darkness, to make something difficult to see through. The Form IV verb أَغْطَشَ means to cause something to become dark. This isn’t just absence of light - it’s active darkening, making things invisible. The word غَطَش also relates to weakness in vision when one walks slowly because they can barely see.

لَيْلَهَا (laylaha) [ل-ي-ل]

The root ل-ي-ل means night, darkness, the time of rest. لَيْل is night, and the attached pronoun “ha” means “its” - referring to the sky’s night. This emphasizes that night belongs to the sky system that Allah created.

أَخْرَجَ (akhraja) [خ-ر-ج]

The root خ-ر-ج means to exit, to come out, to emerge. The Form IV verb أَخْرَجَ means to bring out, to cause to emerge. Allah brings out the morning - He causes it to emerge brilliantly after the darkness. This suggests active management of the day-night cycle.

ضُحَاهَا (duhaha) [ض-ح-و]

The root ض-ح-و refers to the bright morning time, when the sun is at its most brilliant (around 10 AM-noon). الضُّحَى is the time of maximum visibility and light. The attached pronoun “ha” means “its” - the sky’s brilliant morning. This represents the peak of illumination, when everything is perfectly visible.

Ayah 30

الْأَرْضَ (al-arda) [أ-ر-ض]

The root أ-ر-ض means earth, land, ground - the physical surface we live upon. الْأَرْض is THE earth, the planet, the ground beneath our feet. This is being contrasted with السَّمَاء (the sky) mentioned earlier.

بَعْدَ (ba’da) [ب-ع-د]

The root ب-ع-د means after, distance, separation in time. بَعْدَ means “after” or “thereafter.” This indicates sequence - Allah worked on the earth after or alongside the sky.

دَحَاهَا (dahaha) [د-ح-و]

The root د-ح-و means to smooth out, to make even, to spread out for use. This word comes from the image of an ostrich scraping the ground to make it smooth so it can lay its egg. The verb means Allah removed the unevenness and impediments from the earth, making it suitable for travel, settlement, and civilization. This is NOT about making the earth egg-shaped - it’s about making the surface smooth and traversable.

Ayah 31

أَخْرَجَ (akhraja) [خ-ر-ج]

The root خ-ر-ج means to bring out, to extract, to cause to emerge. This is the same verb used for bringing out the morning light (ayah 29). Here it’s used twice - bringing out water and bringing out pasture from the earth.

مَاءَهَا (ma’aha) [م-و-ه]

The root م-و-ه means water, liquid, fluid. مَاء is water, and the attached pronoun “ha” refers to the earth - “its water.” Allah extracted water from the earth - rivers, springs, wells. This was the economic foundation of ancient civilizations, particularly Egypt.

مَرْعَاهَا (mar’aha) [ر-ع-ي]

The root ر-ع-ي means to graze, to pasture, to tend livestock. مَرْعَى is the pasture, grazing land, greenery where animals feed. The attached pronoun “ha” means “its” - the earth’s pasture. This word appears repeatedly in connection with Musa (AS) in the Quran, particularly when he told Pharaoh to graze his animals.

Ayah 32

الْجِبَالَ (al-jibala) [ج-ب-ل]

The root ج-ب-ل means mountain, something firmly established and unmovable. الْجِبَال is the plural - mountains. These represent permanent, stable features of the landscape.

أَرْسَاهَا (arsaha) [ر-س-و]

The root ر-س-و means to anchor, to peg down, to make firm and stable. The Form IV verb أَرْسَى means to firmly establish, to anchor down. Allah pegged the mountains into the earth like stakes, giving stability to the land. This connects to the image of the pyramids - humanity’s attempt to create mountain-like structures.

Ayah 33

مَتَاعًا (mata’an) [م-ت-ع]

The root م-ت-ع means to enjoy, to benefit from, to use temporarily. مَتَاع is temporary provision, something to enjoy for a limited time. This emphasizes that all these resources - water, pasture, mountains - are temporary provisions for this worldly life.

لَّكُمْ (lakum) [ل-ك-م]

The particle لِ with the pronoun كُم means “for you” (plural). This indicates Allah created all of this for humanity’s benefit.

لِأَنْعَامِكُمْ (li-an’amikum) [ن-ع-م]

The root ن-ع-م relates to blessings, softness, comfort. أَنْعَام refers to domesticated livestock - cattle, camels, sheep, goats. These animals that have been “softened” and made subservient to human use. The attached pronoun كُم means “your” - your livestock. By pairing humans and animals in the same phrase, Allah humbles humanity - just as animals are dependent on and subservient to humans, so humans are dependent on and should be subservient to Allah.

Ayah 34

الطَّامَّةُ (at-tammatu) [ط-م-م]

The root ط-م-م means to overflow, to cover completely, to obliterate. الطَّامَّة is the overwhelming calamity, the disaster that destroys everything in its path. It’s used for a tsunami or flood that sweeps away everything. The Kubra (greatest) is added to emphasize this is THE calamity beyond all calamities.

الْكُبْرَىٰ (al-kubra) [ك-ب-ر]

The root ك-ب-ر means great, large, major. الْكُبْرَى is the feminine superlative form meaning “the greatest” or “the major one.” Combined with at-Tammah, this creates the phrase “the Greatest Calamity” - Judgment Day.

Ayah 35

يَتَذَكَّرُ (yatadhakkaru) [ذ-ك-ر]

The root ذ-ك-ر means to remember, to recall, to mention. The Form V verb تَذَكَّرَ means to remember thoroughly, to recall with full awareness. The يَ prefix indicates third person future tense. Human beings will thoroughly remember.

الْإِنسَانُ (al-insanu) [أ-ن-س]

The root أ-ن-س means human being, but it’s related to نِسْيَان (forgetfulness). The human being is the forgetful creature by nature. The irony here is powerful - the forgetful being will finally remember everything.

سَعَىٰ (sa’a) [س-ع-ي]

The root س-ع-ي means to strive, to make effort, to run toward something. سَعَى is the past tense - “he strived.” This encompasses all efforts, priorities, goals, and pursuits that occupied a person in worldly life. Everything you ran after will be recalled.

Ayah 36

بُرِّزَتِ (burrizat) [ب-ر-ز]

The root ب-ر-ز means to emerge, to come out prominently, to be manifest. The Form II verb بَرَّزَ means to make something clearly visible and identifiable. The passive form بُرِّزَت means “it was made manifest.” Hell will emerge fully visible, undeniable, impossible to mistake for anything else.

الْجَحِيمُ (al-jahimu) [ج-ح-م]

The root ج-ح-م means intense fire, blazing flames that build upon flames. جَحِيم is the roaring, raging fire - one of the names of Hell. It suggests a fire that roars like a lion about to attack, coals burning red-hot, flames compounding upon flames. The word also suggests rage and anger - Hell is not just fire, it’s angry fire.

يَرَىٰ (yara) [ر-أ-ي]

The root ر-أ-ي means to see physically with the eyes. Hell will be displayed لِمَن يَرَىٰ - “for whoever sees” - meaning everyone who looks will physically witness it with their own eyes.

Ayah 37

طَغَىٰ (tagha) [ط-غ-ي]

The root ط-غ-ي means to transgress, to rebel, to exceed all bounds. طَغَى means he rebelled, exceeded limits, was tyrannical and oppressive. This describes someone who violates all boundaries, shows no consideration for rules or others, and acts with ruthless disregard. This was Pharaoh’s defining characteristic.

Ayah 38

آثَرَ (athara) [أ-ث-ر]

The root أ-ث-ر means to prefer, to give preference to, but also means “remains” or “traces.” آثَرَ means to choose something thinking it will last, to prefer something you want to hold onto forever. The word captures the attitude of wanting to live only in the moment, to make this life eternal, to remain in worldly pleasure without thinking of consequences.

الْحَيَاةَ (al-hayata) [ح-ي-و]

The root ح-ي-و means life, living, vitality. الْحَيَاة is the life, existence.

الدُّنْيَا (ad-dunya) [د-ن-و]

The root د-ن-و means to be near, low, or close. الدُّنْيَا is the superlative feminine form meaning “the lowest” or “the nearest.” This is the lowest form of life possible - the most base existence compared to the higher levels of life (the seven heavens). It’s the nearest life (close at hand) but the lowest in value.

Ayah 39

الْجَحِيمَ (al-jahima) [ج-ح-م]

Same as ayah 36 - the raging, roaring hellfire.

الْمَأْوَىٰ (al-ma’wa) [أ-و-ي]

The root أ-و-ي means to take refuge, to find shelter, to return to for protection. الْمَأْوَى is the place of refuge, sanctuary, shelter, the place you return to for safety.
